Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
أَحَسِبَ ٱلنَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوٓا۟ أَن يَقُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ (٢)
Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We have faith,’ and they will not be tested? (2)
وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۖ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٣)
Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars. (3)

Content of Surah Al-Ankabut

The prevailing view among a group of researchers is that this entire Surah was revealed in Makkah; accordingly, its content is consistent with the themes of Makkan Surahs. It speaks of the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection, the uprising of the great Prophets of the past and their struggle against the polytheists and tyrannical, oppressive idol-worshippers, as well as their ultimate victory and the destruction of those oppressive groups.

It discusses the call toward the Truth, divine trials, and the excuses sought by the disbelievers in various contexts.

Although some have excepted the first eleven verses of this Surah, believing they were revealed in Madinah—their motivation for this belief perhaps being certain occasions of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) that we will mention later, as well as the discussion of Jihad mentioned in these verses and the reference to the hypocrites (Munafiqin) in some of them, which are characteristics suited to Madinan Surahs.

However, we will see later that these matters do not contradict the "Makkan" nature of the Surah. In any case, the naming of this Surah as "Surah Al-Ankabut" (The Spider) is taken from verse 41, which likens the idol-worshippers—who rely on other than Allah—to a spider, whose reliance is upon frail and baseless webs.

Generally, it can be said that the discussions in this Surah are summarized in four sections:

  1. The opening section of the Surah, which revolves around the issue of "Trial" (Imtihan) and the "State of the Hypocrites." These two are inseparably linked, as the identification of hypocrites is impossible except within the storms of trials and tests.

  2. Another section which is, in fact, intended to comfort the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the few early believers by recounting aspects of the fate of great Prophets such as Nuh, Ibrahim, Lut, and Shu'ayb (peace be upon them). They stood against tyrants like Nimrod and self-indulgent wealthy elites; the tools of this struggle, the nature of the struggle, and its conclusion are specified to serve as both a consolation for the believers and a warning for the cold-hearted and oppressive polytheists of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) era.

  3. Another section of this Surah, particularly located toward the end, speaks of Monotheism (Tawhid), the signs of Allah in the world of creation, and the struggle against polytheism, calling upon human conscience and primordial nature (Fitrah) to judge.

  4. Another part of this Surah revolves around diverse topics concerning the weakness and inability of fabricated deities and their "spider-like" worshippers. It also discusses the greatness of the Quran, the proofs of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam, the stubbornness of the opponents, and a series of educational matters such as prayer (Salah), kindness to parents, righteous deeds, and the method of logical discussion and interaction with opponents.

The Virtue of this Surah

In Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family):

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Ankabut shall have a reward of ten good deeds multiplied by the number of all believers and hypocrites."

Specifically, regarding the recitation of Surah Al-Ankabut and Surah Ar-Rum during the month of Ramadan on the twenty-third night, an extraordinary virtue has been narrated, to the extent that we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him):

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Ankabut and Ar-Rum in the month of Ramadan on the twenty-third night, by Allah, he is among the people of Paradise; I make no exception for anyone in this matter, and I do not fear that Allah will record a sin against me for this decisive oath of mine. Indeed, these two Surahs hold a high station before Allah." [1]

Undoubtedly, the rich content of these two Surahs, their important lessons on Monotheism, and the constructive practical programs presented in them are sufficient to lead any person of reflection, inspiration, and action toward eternal Paradise.

In fact, perhaps if we only take inspiration from the first verse of Surah Al-Ankabut, we would be included in the oath of Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—the very verse that raises the issue of the universal trial of humanity and says: everyone, without exception, is placed in the crucible of tests so that those who are impure may be exposed.

How is it possible for a person to fully believe in this great trial and not prepare himself for it, nor become a person of piety and God-wariness?

[1] "Thawab al-A'mal" (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 147) — It is noteworthy that we are writing this part of the Tafsir exactly on the eve of the 23rd night of Ramadan, 1403 AH.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

Alif, Lam, Mim. (1) Do the people think that they will be left alone because they say, "We believe," and they will not be tested? (2) And We certainly tested those before them, and Allah will surely make evident those who are truthful, and He will surely make evident the liars. (3)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated a report according to which the "eleven verses" at the beginning of this Surah were revealed in Madinah regarding Muslims who were in Makkah and professed Islam but were unwilling to migrate to Madinah. They received a letter from their brothers in Madinah stating: "Allah does not accept your profession of faith unless you migrate and come to us." They decided to migrate and left Makkah; a group of polytheists pursued them and fought them. Some were killed and some escaped (and possibly some surrendered and returned to Makkah).

Others consider the "second verse" to be about Ammar ibn Yasir and a group of other early Muslims who believed and were severely tortured by the enemies.

Some have said the eighth verse was revealed regarding the conversion of "Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas" to Islam.

However, an examination of these verses themselves shows that there is no evidence within them linking the verses to the Migration (Hijrah). They merely describe the pressures exerted on the believers at that time by enemies, and sometimes even by their polytheistic parents.

These verses encourage the Muslims to be steadfast and firm against the wave of enemy pressure. If Jihad is mentioned, it apparently refers to Jihad in this context, not the collective armed Jihad, the command for which was revealed in Madinah.

Furthermore, if it speaks of hypocrites, it may refer to the weak-of-faith group that occasionally existed among the Muslims in Makkah—sometimes siding with the Muslims and sometimes with the polytheists, turning toward whichever side became heavier! In any case, the cohesion and integration of the verses of this Surah require us to consider all of them "Makkan," and the aforementioned reports, which are inconsistent with one another, cannot break this cohesion.

Commentary

Divine Trial is an Eternal Sunnah

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the disjointed letters (Alif-Lam-Mim), the interpretation of which we have explained many times from various perspectives. [1]

[1] Refer to the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah in Volume 1, the beginning of Surah Al-Imran in Volume 2, and the beginning of Surah Al-A'raf in Volume 6.

After mentioning the disjointed letters, it points to one of the most important issues of human life: the issue of hardships, pressures, and divine trials.

First, it says: "Do the people think that they will be left alone just because they profess faith and testify to Monotheism and the Messenger's mission, and they will not be tested?" (أَ حَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَ هُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ) [1]

Immediately after, it proceeds to mention the reality that trial is a constant and eternal Divine Sunnah (law). Trial is not exclusive to you, the Muslim community; it is a law that has been applied to all previous nations. It says: "And We certainly tested those before them" (وَ لَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

They too were cast into the harsh furnaces of trial; they too, like you, were under the pressure of ruthless, ignorant, uninformed, fanatical, and stubborn enemies. The field of trial has always been open, and groups have participated in this arena.

It must be so, because in the stage of claim, anyone can present themselves as the foremost believer, the highest mujahid, and the most self-sacrificing human. The weight, price, and value of these claims must be clarified through testing. It must become known to what extent inner intentions and spiritual readiness are aligned or misaligned with these words.

Yes, "Allah must know those who are truthful, and He must know the liars" (فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَ لَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكاذِبِينَ).

[1] "Yuftanun" is derived from "Fitnah," which originally means placing gold in fire to determine its level of purity. Subsequently, it has been applied to any form of outward or spiritual trial (for further explanation, see Vol. 2, p. 17).

It is obvious that Allah knows all of this, even before the creation of humans. The meaning of "knowledge" here is the objective realization of matters and their external existence—or in other words, the manifestation of practical effects and evidence. That is, Allah's knowledge regarding this group must be implemented practically in the external world and achieve objective reality, and everyone must bring out what they have within. This is the meaning of "knowledge" when used regarding Allah in such instances.

The reason for this is also clear: inner intentions and internal attributes do not carry the meaning of reward, recompense, or punishment until they are realized and objectified in human action.

Trial is for the purpose of giving reality to inner intentions and attributes.

In other words, this world is like a "university" or a "farm" (these metaphors appear in the texts of Islamic traditions). The program is for talents to flourish in the university, for merits to be nurtured, and for what is in the stage of "potential" to reach "actuality."

In this farm, the essence of the seeds must appear, and sprouts must emerge from within them. The sprouts must rise from the soil, be nurtured, become small saplings, and finally a sturdy and fruitful tree—and these matters are never possible without testing and trial.

From this, we understand that divine trials are not for the purpose of identifying individuals, but rather for the nurturing and flourishing of talents.

Therefore, if we conduct a test, it is to discover something unknown; but if Allah tests, it is not to discover an unknown—as His knowledge encompasses everything—but rather to nurture talents and bring potentials into actuality. [1]

[1] Further explanation regarding Divine Trial and various aspects of this subject is provided in Volume 1 under verse 157 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Key Points 1- Trials in Different Forms

Although stating the universality of trial for all nations and groups had a very significant constructive effect on the believers in Makkah—who were in a severe minority at that time—and awareness of this reality made them highly resistant and patient against enemies, this was not limited to the believers of Makkah. Rather, every group and community shares in this Divine Sunnah in some way, and Allah's trials come to them in various forms.

Some groups are placed in environments that are polluted in every respect, where temptations of corruption surround them from all sides. Their great trial is not to take on the color of the environment in such an atmosphere and conditions, and to preserve their original purity.

Some groups are placed under the pressure of deprivation, while they see that if they were willing to trade the fundamental assets of their being, poverty and deprivation would quickly break; but at the cost of losing faith, piety, freedom, dignity, and honor—and this is their test.

Another group, conversely, is drowned in blessings, and material possibilities are provided for them in every respect. In such conditions, do they rise to the duty of gratitude for the blessing? Or do they become drowned in negligence, arrogance, selfishness, and vanity, immersed in pleasures and lusts, and alienated from society and themselves?!

Another group—like the Westernized or Easternized people of our time—encounters countries that, while distant from God, virtue, and morality, enjoy a dazzling material civilization and significant social welfare. Here, a mysterious, powerful attraction draws them toward this type of life—to provide such a life for themselves and their society at the cost of trampling all the principles they believe in and at the cost of submitting to the humiliation of dependency. This too is a type of test.

Calamities, pain and suffering, wars and conflicts, famine, high prices and inflation, autocratic governments that invite humans to their slavery and captivity and call them to submit to their Taghuti programs, and finally the powerful waves of whims and lusts—each of these is a means of trial on the path of Allah's servants. It is in these scenes that the faith, personality, piety, purity, trustworthiness, and freedom of individuals are identified.

However, for victory in these harsh trials, there is no way except continuous effort and reliance on the special grace of the Lord.

Interestingly, in a Hadith found in Usul al-Kafi from some of the Infallibles regarding the interpretation of the verse "Do the people think that they will be left alone because they say, 'We believe,' and they will not be tested?" we read:

"They are tested just as gold is tested in a furnace, and they are purified just as the pressure of fire removes the impurities of gold and makes it pure!" [1]

In any case, those seekers of ease who imagine that merely professing faith will place them in the ranks of the believers and that they will be companions of the Prophets, the Truthful, and the Martyrs in the highest reaches of Paradise are gravely mistaken.

As Commander of the Faithful Ali (peace be upon him) says in Nahj al-Balagha:

"By Him who sent the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) with the truth, you shall surely be confounded, and you shall surely be sifted, and you shall surely be stirred as with a ladle in a pot, until your lowest becomes your highest and your highest becomes your lowest!" [2]

He spoke these words when the people had just pledged allegiance to him and were waiting to see what he would do with the distribution of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and positions—whether according to past standards and discrimination, or according to the bitter standard of Muhammadi justice?!

[1] "Usul al-Kafi" as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 148. [2] Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 16.

Ayahs

أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن يَسْبِقُونَا ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٤)
Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outmaneuver Us? Evil is the judgement that they make. (4)
مَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ ٱللَّهِ لَـَٔاتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٥)
Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] time will indeed come, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (5)
وَمَن جَـٰهَدَ فَإِنَّمَا يُجَـٰهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦)
Whoever strives, strives only for his own sake. Indeed Allah has no need of the creatures. (6)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَحْسَنَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٧)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will absolve them of their misdeeds and We will surely reward them by the best of what they used to do. (7)

Commentary

Escaping the Sphere of Allah's Power is Impossible!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the general testing of the believers. The first verse under discussion serves as a severe warning to the disbelievers and sinners, so they do not imagine that if they pressure the believers and Divine punishments do not immediately overtake them, that Allah is heedless of them or lacks the power to punish them.

He says: "Or do those who practice evil deeds think they can outstrip Us and escape the grasp of Our retribution? Evil is the judgment they make!" (أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ يَسْبِقُونا ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ).

Let the Divine respite not deceive them, for this too is a trial for them and an opportunity for repentance and return.

The fact that some commentators have considered the above verse to refer to sinful believers is by no means consistent with the context of the verses; rather, the evidence testifies that the intended meaning is the polytheists and disbelievers.

Then, once again, He returns to the programs of the believers and offers them counsel, saying: "Whoever hopes for the meeting with Allah, let him not withhold any effort in obeying His command, for the term appointed by Allah is surely coming" (مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللَّهِ لَآتٍ) [1].

[1]: This sentence actually has an omitted part, and its estimation is as follows: "Whoever hopes for the meeting with Allah, let him hasten to obedience before the term reaches him"—or—"Whoever hopes for the meeting with Allah and says 'I believe in Allah,' let him say it steadfastly and patiently... for the term of Allah is coming."

Yes, this Divine promise is infallible, and it is a path that must be traversed in any case.

Furthermore, Allah hears your words and is aware of your deeds and intentions, for "He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

Regarding what is meant by "Liqa' Allah" (Meeting with Allah), some have interpreted it as meeting the angels of the Lord, some as meeting the reckoning and reward, and some as meeting the decree and command of the Truth. Others have considered it an allusion to the Day of Judgment and the Resurrection. However, there is no reason to interpret the verse with these metaphorical meanings.

It must be said: The meeting with the Lord on the Day of Resurrection is not a sensory meeting, but rather a spiritual meeting and a type of inner witnessing. This is because there, the thick veils of the material world are removed from before the eye of the human soul, and a state of witnessing occurs for the human being.

As stated by "Allamah Tabataba'i" in Al-Mizan, the meaning of "Liqa' Allah" is that the servants will stand in a position where there is no veil between them and their Lord, because the nature of the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation of realities, as the Quran says: وَ يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ: "That day they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth" (Surah An-Nur, Verse 25). [1]

The next verse is, in fact, a justification for what preceded in the previous verse. It says: The reason it was commanded that those who hope for the meeting with Allah should not withhold any effort within their power

[1]: Regarding the interpretation of "Liqa' Allah," we have another discussion in Volume 1 under Verse 46 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

is because "Whoever strives (performs Jihad) and exerts effort and bears hardships and difficulties, in reality, strives for himself, for Allah is free of need from all the worlds" (وَ مَنْ جاهَدَ فَإِنَّما يُجاهِدُ لِنَفْسِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَغَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ).

The program of Divine testing, the struggle against the whims of the self, and the battle against stubborn enemies to preserve faith, purity, and piety, is a program for the human being himself. Otherwise, Allah is a Being who is infinite in every respect and has no need that could be fulfilled through the worship or obedience of His servants. He has no deficiency that others could provide for Him; rather, whatever others have, they have from Him, and they possess nothing of their own.

From this explanation, it becomes clear that "Jihad" here does not necessarily mean armed struggle with the enemy, but rather carries its original linguistic meaning, which encompasses every kind of effort and striving to preserve faith and piety, and enduring various hardships and local struggles against a stubborn and spiteful enemy.

In short, all the benefits of this Jihad return to the person of the Mujahid (striver), and it is he who attains the good of this world and the Hereafter in the light of his Jihad. Even if society benefits from the blessings of this Jihad, it will be in the subsequent stage. Therefore, whenever the success of this Jihad is granted to someone, he must thank Allah for this great blessing.

The last verse under discussion is an explanation and completion of what was mentioned concisely in the previous verse under the title of "Jihad." Here, it unfolds the reality of Jihad and states: "Those who believe and perform righteous deeds, We shall surely expiate their sins" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ). Therefore, the first benefit of this great Jihad (faith and righteous deeds) is the expiation

and covering of sins, which accrues to the human being himself, just as the reward for it belongs to them as well, as the Quran says at the end of this same verse: "We shall certainly reward them according to the best of what they used to do" (وَ لَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَحْسَنَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

"Nukaffir" is from the root "Takfir," which originally means "to cover," and the meaning of covering sins here is Divine pardon and forgiveness.

The expression "the best of what they used to do" (أَحْسَنَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ), despite the fact that Allah rewards all good deeds whether they are "good" (Hasan) or "best" (Ahsan), may be an indication that We count all their good deeds according to the best of them. That is, if some of their deeds are excellent and some are good or average, We count them all as excellent. This is the meaning of the Lord's grace (Fadl), which is also mentioned in other verses of the Quran (such as Verse 38 of An-Nur): لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَ يَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ: "That Allah may reward them for the best of what they did and increase for them out of His grace."

Ayahs

وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حُسْنًا ۖ وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَآ ۚ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
We have enjoined man to be good to his parents. But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. To Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do. (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٩)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will surely admit them among the righteous. (9)

Occasion of Revelation

Various narrations have been cited regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verse, the essence of all of which is the same: "Some men who were in Mecca accepted faith and Islam [1]. When their mothers became aware of this matter, they decided not to eat food or drink water until their sons returned from Islam! Although none of these mothers fulfilled their word and eventually broke their hunger strike, the above verse was revealed

[1]: In some narrations, the name "Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas" is mentioned, and in others, the name "Ayyash ibn Abi Rabi'ah al-Makhzumi."

and provided a clear guideline for everyone regarding the interaction with parents in the context of faith and disbelief."

Commentary

The Supreme Recommendation Regarding Parents

One of the most important divine trials is the "conflict" between the path of faith and piety and the bonds of affection and kinship. In this regard, the Quran has clearly defined the duty of Muslims.

First, as a general law originating from the roots of emotion and gratitude, It says: "And We have enjoined upon man goodness to his parents" (وَ وَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ حُسْناً).

Although this is a legislative decree, before being a legislative necessity, this matter exists as a primordial law in the nature of all human beings. The use of the term "man" (human) here is particularly noteworthy, as this law is not exclusive to believers; rather, anyone worthy of the name "human" must be grateful toward their parents and must not forget to respect, honor, and be kind to them throughout their lives, even though through these actions one can never fully repay their debt to them.

Then, so that no one imagines that the emotional bond with parents can override the bond between man and Allah and the matter of faith, It clarifies the subject with an explicit exception, saying: "But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them" (وَ إِنْ جاهَداكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهُما).

The expression "جاهَداكَ" (they endeavor/strive against you) implies their use of utmost effort, persistence, and insistence.

And the expression "ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ" (that of which you have no knowledge) points to the irrationality of polytheism (shirk); for if polytheism were truly correct, there would be evidence for it. In other words, where a person has no knowledge of something, they should not follow it, let alone when they have knowledge of its falsehood.

Following such a thing is following ignorance. If parents compel you to follow ignorance, do not obey them. Fundamentally, blind imitation is wrong, even if it were in the case of faith, let alone in polytheism and disbelief.

The same recommendation regarding parents appears in Surah Luqman, with the addition that it says there: وَ صاحِبْهُما فِي الدُّنْيا مَعْرُوفاً: "While you do not accept their call to polytheism, treat them with kindness in the affairs of this world and behave well in your association with them." Lest anyone imagine that opposing parents regarding the call to polytheism is a justification for mistreating them; this proves the ultimate emphasis of Islam regarding respect for parents.

Thus, a general principle is derived from here: nothing can override the relationship between man and Allah, which takes precedence over everything, even the bond with parents, which is the closest of emotional ties.

The famous Hadith:

لا طاعة لمخلوق فى معصية الخالق:

"There is no obedience to a created being in disobedience to the Creator," which is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, provides a clear criterion in these matters [1].

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 165.

At the end of the verse, It adds: "To Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do," and I will place your reward and punishment at your disposal without any deficiency (إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

This sentence is, in fact, a threat to those who pursue the path of polytheism and those who invite others to this path, for it explicitly states: Allah keeps the account of all their deeds and will deliver it to them at the appropriate time.

The next verse once again repeats and emphasizes the truth previously stated regarding those who have faith and perform righteous deeds, saying: "And those who believe and do righteous deeds, We will surely admit them among the righteous" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُدْخِلَنَّهُمْ فِي الصَّالِحِينَ).

Fundamentally, a person's actions give them their character; righteous deeds spiritually color a person with their own hue and enter them into the ranks of the "righteous," while evil deeds place one in the ranks of the wicked and the "unrighteous."

Regarding the purpose of this repetition, some have said that the previous verses referred to those who tread the path of truth, while here it refers to those who are the guides of this path and the leaders of the way of monotheism, as the term "righteous" (salihin) has been used for many prophets who asked Allah to join them with the righteous.

It is also possible that in the previous verses, the talk was about the forgiveness of sins and the worthy reward for this group of believers, but here it refers to their high station, which is itself another reward; they are placed in the rank of the righteous, in the rank of the prophets, the truthful, and the martyrs, and are their companions and associates.

Key Point: Kindness to Parents

This is not the first time the Quran mentions this important human issue; it was previously mentioned in Surah Al-Isra, verse 23, and will be mentioned again in Surah Luqman, verses 14 and 15, and Surah Al-Ahqaf, verse 15.

In truth, Islam accords the highest respect to these two individuals, such that even if they are polytheists and invite toward polytheism—which is the most detestable act in the view of Islam—it still considers maintaining respect for them obligatory, while simultaneously rejecting their call to polytheism.

This is indeed one of the great divine trials mentioned at the beginning of this Surah, for sometimes they reach years of age where caring for and enduring them becomes difficult; it is here that children must pass their test in terms of gratitude and obedience to Allah's command, and look after their fathers and mothers in the best possible way.

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: A person came to him and asked:

"To whom should I be kind?" He said: "To your mother." The person asked again: "Who after her?" He said: "To your mother." He asked a third time: "Who after her?"

Again He said: "To your mother!" And on the fourth time, He recommended the father and then other relatives in order of their closeness to the person [1]. In another Hadith found in many books, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

الجنة تحت اقدام الامهات:

"Paradise is under the feet of mothers" (and only through humility and being like the dust of the path before them can one find the way to the highest Paradise) [2].

[1] and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ فَإِذَآ أُوذِىَ فِى ٱللَّهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ ٱلنَّاسِ كَعَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَئِن جَآءَ نَصْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّا كُنَّا مَعَكُمْ ۚ أَوَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِمَا فِى صُدُورِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠)
Among the people there are those who say, ‘We have faith in Allah,’ but if such a one is tormented in Allah’s cause, he takes persecution by the people for Allah’s punishment. Yet if there comes any help from your Lord, they will say, ‘We were indeed with you.’ Does not Allah know best what is in the breasts of the creatures? (10)
وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ (١١)
Allah shall surely ascertain those who have faith, and He shall surely ascertain the hypocrites. (11)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ سَبِيلَنَا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطَـٰيَـٰكُمْ وَمَا هُم بِحَـٰمِلِينَ مِنْ خَطَـٰيَـٰهُم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٢)
The faithless say to the faithful, ‘Follow our way and we will bear [responsibility for] your iniquities.’ They will not bear anything of their iniquities. They are indeed liars. (12)
وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقَالَهُمْ وَأَثْقَالًا مَّعَ أَثْقَالِهِمْ ۖ وَلَيُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣)
But they will carry their own burdens and other burdens along with their own burdens, and they will surely be questioned on the Day of Resurrection concerning that which they used to fabricate. (13)

Commentary

Partners in Victory, but Not in Hardships!

Since the previous verses explicitly discussed "righteous believers" and "polytheists," the first of the verses under discussion addresses the third group, namely the "hypocrites," stating: "Some people profess faith, yet they show no endurance or steadfastness against the pressure of opponents. When they are persecuted in the way of Allah, they abandon their faith and regard the persecution and torture of people as if it were the punishment of Allah" (and they are deeply terrified by it) (وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللَّهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللَّهِ).

"But when victory comes from your Lord and you are triumphant, they say: 'We were with you and we are partners in your honors!'" (وَلَئِنْ جاءَ نَصْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّا كُنَّا مَعَكُمْ).

Do they imagine that Allah is unaware of the depths of their hearts? "Is not Allah best aware of what is in the bosoms of all the worlds' inhabitants?" (أَوَلَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِأَعْلَمَ بِما فِي صُدُورِ الْعالَمِينَ).

The use of the expression "آمنا" (We have believed) in the plural form, while the subsequent phrase is in the singular, might be because this group of hypocrites wants to insert themselves into the ranks of the community of believers; thus, they say "آمنا," meaning "we have believed just as other people have."

The expression "أُوذِيَ فِي اللَّهِ" means "persecuted in the way of Allah," implying that they are sometimes harassed by the enemy for the sake of God and faith.

Interestingly, the verse refers to divine punishment as "عذاب" (torment) and to the harassment by people as "فتنة" (trial/tribulation), indicating that the harassment by people is not truly a punishment, but rather a test and a means of perfection for human beings. In this way, He teaches them not to compare the two, and not to abandon their faith under the pretext that opponents are harassing and torturing them, for this is part of the general program of testing in this world.

A question arises here: What victory had Allah granted the Muslims in Mecca that the hypocrites would consider themselves participants in?

In response, we say: The above sentence is in the form of a "conditional" statement, and we know that a conditional sentence does not prove the existence of the condition; rather, its meaning is that if victories are granted to you in the future, these weak-faithed hypocrites will consider themselves partners in them.

Furthermore, even in Mecca, the Muslims achieved victories against the enemies, even if they were not military victories; rather, they were victories in propagation, influencing public opinion, and the progress of Islam among various strata of people.

Beyond all this, the expression regarding the persecution and harassment of believers is consistent with the environment of Mecca; otherwise, such things rarely occurred in the environment of Medina.

Additionally, this point also became clear: "hypocrite" does not only refer to those who have no faith in their hearts at all and merely profess it; rather, weak-faithed individuals who quickly change their beliefs under the pressure of others are also considered among the hypocrites. The verse under discussion apparently speaks of this type of hypocrite and explicitly states that Allah is aware of their intentions.

In the next verse, to emphasize further, He adds: "And Allah most certainly knows those who believe, and He most certainly knows the hypocrites" (وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ). If simple-minded people think they can remain outside the realm of Allah's knowledge by hiding truths, they are gravely mistaken.

We repeat again: the use of the term "منافق" (hypocrite) is not evidence that these verses were revealed in Medina. It is true that the issue of hypocrisy usually arises after the victory of a group and their taking over the government—when opponents change their appearance and form underground groups—but as we have said, hypocrisy has a broad meaning and includes weak-faithed individuals who change their beliefs with the slightest pressure.

The next verse points to an example of the weak and hollow logic of the polytheists, which still exists among a wide segment of people today, saying: "The disbelievers said to the believers: 'Follow our way and our creed, and if there be any sin, we will bear your sins upon ourselves'" (وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبِعُوا سَبِيلَنا وَلْنَحْمِلْ خَطاياكُمْ) [1].

[1]: The phrase "وَلْنَحْمِلْ" is an imperative verb, which has raised an objection for some commentators: can a person command themselves? They then answered that this command is in the sense of a conditional proposition, meaning "if you follow us, we will bear your sins" (Tafsir Fakhr Razi). However, in our view, there is no obstacle to a person commanding themselves; the commander and the commanded are one person here, but in two different capacities (reflect on this).

Today, too, we see many tempters who, when inviting someone to a wrong act, say, "If there is any sin, it is on our necks!" while we know that no one can bear the sin of another, and such a thing is not rational at all. Allah is Just; He does not punish one person for the crime of another. Furthermore, human responsibility for one's actions does not vanish with these baseless words. Contrary to what some narrow-minded people imagine, these expressions do not reduce a person's punishment by a hair's breadth; therefore, no court or tribunal pays attention to such claims that "so-and-so has taken the sin upon himself." It is true that this encourager is a partner in the sin and crime, but this partnership in crime will in no way diminish the perpetrator's responsibility.

Therefore, in the next sentence, He explicitly states: "They will never bear anything of their errors and sins; indeed, they are liars" (وَما هُمْ بِحامِلِينَ مِنْ خَطاياهُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

A question arises here: truth and falsehood apply to declarative sentences, whereas in our discussion, there is no declarative sentence, but rather an optative/imperative sentence (a command), and we know that such sentences do not possess truth or falsehood. So why does the Quran say they are lying?

The answer to this question becomes clear from the previous explanation: the imperative sentence here reverts to a conditional declarative sentence, and its meaning is: "If you follow our path, we will bear your sins," and such a sentence is capable of being true or false [1].

[1]: We have another way to answer this question as well, for we believe that truth and falsehood also find their way into optative/imperative sentences, as seen in common usage. For instance, when a person commands something, it is evidence that they truly desire it; when we say "he is lying," it means he does not actually want such a thing (reflect on this).

Then, so it is not imagined that these inviters to disbelief, polytheism, idolatry, and oppression face no punishment for this act of theirs, He adds in the next verse: "They shall certainly carry their own heavy burdens of sins, and other burdens along with their own burdens!" (وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالًا مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ). This additional burden of sin is the burden of the sin of misguiding, seducing, and encouraging others to sin—the same burden of establishing an evil tradition, as the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Whoever establishes an evil tradition, upon him is its sin and the sin of those who act upon it, without anything being diminished from their sins." [1] The important point is that they are partners in all the sins of others as well, without a hair's breadth being diminished from the others' sins.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "They will most certainly be questioned on the Day of Resurrection concerning the fabrications and lies they used to forge" (وَلَيُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ عَمَّا كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ).

Here another question arises: what is meant by this "fabrication" for which they must answer on the Day of Resurrection?

It might refer to the lies they forged against Allah, saying: "Allah has commanded us to worship these idols!" Or it might refer to the fact that by saying "we will bear your sins," they meant that these acts are not sins at all, and this is a lie for which they must answer.

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, vol. 25, p. 40.

Or it could be that on the Day of Resurrection, they will truly be told, "Come and bear the burden of their sins," and they will refuse, thus revealing their lie and fabrication.

Or it could be that the outward meaning of their words was that any human can take responsibility for the sin of another, while this statement is also a lie and fabrication, as everyone is responsible for their own actions.

Key Points

1. Good and Evil Traditions

In Islamic logic, establishing a social program creates responsibility and inevitably makes a person a partner and participant in that program and in the work of all those who act upon it. This is because the motives for an action are part of the preliminaries of the action, and we know that whoever is involved in the preliminary of a task is also a partner in the outcome, even if the preliminary is simple.

Evidence for this is a hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was with a group of companions when a beggar came and asked for help. No one gave him anything. A man stepped forward and gave him something; others were encouraged and they also gave. At this point, the Prophet said:

"Whoever establishes a good tradition and it is followed, for him is its reward and the rewards of those who followed him, without anything being diminished from their rewards. And whoever establishes an evil tradition and it is followed, upon him is its sin and the sins of those who followed him, without anything being diminished from their sins!" [1] Similar meanings have appeared in various wordings in both Shi'a and Sunni hadith sources, and this is a famous hadith.

2. Answering a Question

Some have raised the question here that in Islamic laws, the blood money (diyah) of a person is sometimes the responsibility of another; for example, in cases of pure accidental killing, the blood money is the responsibility of the "Aqilah" (referring to the male paternal relatives among whom the blood money for an accident is divided, each paying a portion).

Does this issue contradict the above verses?

In response, we say: in jurisprudential discussions, we have clarified this reality—that the liability of the Aqilah is actually a type of mutual and mandatory insurance among the members of a family. To ensure that the heavy burden of accidental blood money does not fall on a single individual, Islam has mandated that the men of a family be guarantors for one another's accidental blood money and distribute the amount among themselves. Today this one might commit an error, and tomorrow another (we defer further explanation of this issue to the discussion of Diyat in books of jurisprudence).

In any case, this program is a type of cooperation and collaboration in preserving mutual interests, and it in no way carries the concept of bearing the sins of others, especially since the blood money for accidental killing is not a penalty for a sin at all, but rather compensation for loss (reflect on this).

[1]: Tafsir "Durr al-Manthur".

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٤)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained with them for a thousand-less-fifty years. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers. (14)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٥)
Then We delivered him and those who were in the Ark, and made it a sign for all the nations. (15)
وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۖ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٦)
And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him. That is better for you, should you know. (16)
إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا فَٱبْتَغُوا۟ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرِّزْقَ وَٱعْبُدُوهُ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۖ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٧)
What you worship instead of Allah are mere idols, and you invent a lie. Indeed those whom you worship besides Allah have no control over your provision. So seek all [your] provision from Allah, and worship Him and thank Him, and to Him you shall be brought back.’ (17)
وَإِن تُكَذِّبُوا۟ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٨)
If you impugn [the Apostle’s teaching], then [other] nations have impugned [likewise] before you, and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (18)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (١٩)
Have they not regarded how Allah originates the creation? Then He will bring it back. That is indeed easy for Allah. (19)

Commentary

An Allusion to the Stories of Noah and Abraham

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 16; p. 228

Where the previous discussions spoke of the general testing of humanity, from here onward, the text reflects upon portions of the severe trials faced by the Prophets and past nations—how they were subjected to pressure and harassment by enemies, how they persevered, and how victory was ultimately granted to them. This serves both as a consolation for the companions of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who were under the pressure of powerful enemies in Mecca during those difficult days, and as a threat to the enemies to be wary of the painful end of their lives.

It begins first with the first of the Ulu al-'Azm (Arch-Prophets), namely Noah عليه السلام, and in a brief statement, recounts that part of his life which was most relevant to the situation of the Muslims of that day.

It says: "And We certainly sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them a thousand years less fifty years" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا).

Night and day, he was occupied with preaching and inviting toward Monotheism (Tawhid). In private and solitude, among the crowds, and by utilizing every opportunity, he called them toward Allah during this long period—meaning 950 years! He did not grow weary of this persistent effort, nor did he allow weakness or lethargy to overcome him. Yet, despite all this, only a small group (about eighty people according to historical reports) believed (meaning, on average, one person every twelve years!).

Therefore, do not grow weary in the path of inviting toward the Truth and struggling against deviations, for your program is easy and simple compared to the program of Noah عليه السلام.

But see where the end of this oppressive and stubborn people led: "Then the flood seized them while they were wrongdoers" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ الطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَالِمُونَ).

In this manner, the scroll of their disgraceful lives was rolled up, and their palaces, castles, and lifeless bodies were buried in the waves of the storm.

The expression "a thousand years less fifty years"—while it was possible to say 950 years from the start—is to point to the greatness and length of this duration, because the number "thousand," especially in the form of "a thousand years" for the "duration of preaching," is considered a very large number.

The apparent meaning of the above verse is that this amount was not the entirety of Noah's life—even though the current Torah mentions this number for the total duration of Noah's life (Torah, Genesis, Chapter 9)—rather, he lived for another period after the flood, which according to some commentators was three hundred years.

Of course, this long lifespan is very great by the standards of lifespans in our time and does not seem natural at all. It is possible that the rate of lifespan in those days differed from today; fundamentally, the people of Noah, as derived from some records, had long lifespans, and among them, Noah himself was extraordinary. Furthermore, this shows that the physical structure of the human being allows for the possibility of a long life.

Modern scientific studies have also shown that human life does not have a fixed and definite limit, and the fact that some have limited it to 120 years, or more or less, is completely baseless; rather, with the change of conditions, it is entirely possible for it to be altered.

Even now, through experiments, they have been able to increase the lifespan of certain plants or other living creatures to twelve times the normal lifespan, and in some cases—if you are not surprised—to nine hundred times! If they succeed in increasing the human lifespan by this same standard, it is possible for a human to live for thousands of years 1. Additionally, it should be noted that the word "Tufan" (flood/storm) originally means any event that surrounds a person (from the root tawaf); subsequently, it was applied to abundant water or a severe flood that covers a large area of land and submerges it. It is also applied to anything severe and abundant that is all-encompassing—whether wind, fire, or water—and sometimes it has also come to mean the intense darkness of the night [2]. Interestingly, it says: وَهُمْ ظَالِمُونَ (while they were wrongdoers), meaning they continued their oppression and wrongdoing even at the moment the flood occurred, hinting that if they had abandoned this act, repented, and come toward Allah, they would never have been caught in such a fate.

1: For further explanation on the secret of longevity—in connection with discussions regarding the long life of Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام—refer to the book Mahdi: The Great Revolutionary.

[2]: Mufradat of Raghib and Farhang-e-Amid.

In the next verse, it adds: "So We saved him and the companions of the ship, and We made it a sign for the worlds" (فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَصْحَابَ السَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَاهَا آيَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ) 1.

Then, following the condensed story of Noah عليه السلام and his people, it moves to the story of Abraham عليه السلام, the second great Ulu al-'Azm Prophet, and says: "And [We sent] Abraham, when he said to his people, 'Worship Allah and fear Him. That is best for you, if you should know'" (وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ ذَلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ) [2]. Here, two important ideological and practical programs of the Prophets—which were the invitation to "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and "Piety" (Taqwa)—are stated together, and at the end, it says: If you think correctly, following Monotheism and Piety is better for you, as it saves your world from the pollutions of polytheism, sin, and misery, and your Hereafter will be eternal happiness.

1: Regarding what the pronoun in "جَعَلْنَاهَا" (We made it) refers to, commentators have given many possibilities. Some have said it refers to the entirety of this event and incident; some consider it an allusion only to the salvation of Noah and his companions; and some have taken it to refer to the "ship," and the apparent wording of the above verse supports this latter possibility. Truly, this ship, especially in that era and time and in such a great incident, was a sign among the signs of Allah.

[2]: "Abraham" here is apparently conjoined to "Noah," and its verb is "We sent" (arsalna); some have taken it as conjoined to the object of "We saved" (anjayna), and some consider it the object of the omitted verb "Mention" (udhkur).

Then Abraham عليه السلام proceeds to the proofs of the futility of idol worship and, with several different expressions—each containing a reason—strongly condemns their creed.

First, he says: "You only worship idols besides Allah" (إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْثَانًا).

The same idols that are lifeless statues—statues without will, without intellect or consciousness, and lacking everything—the very appearance of which is a clear proof of the futility of the belief in idol worship (Note that "awthan" is the plural of "wathan"—on the weight of sanam—meaning stones that they would carve and worship).

After this, he goes further and says: Not only does the state of these idols show they are not deities, but you also know that "you invent a falsehood [by calling them gods]" (وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا).

What proof do you have for this great lie? Nothing but a handful of delusions and superstitions!

Since "takhluqun" is from the root "khalq," which sometimes means creating and making and sometimes means lying, some commentators have mentioned another interpretation for this sentence other than what we stated above; they said the meaning is that you carve and create these idols (these fake deities) with your own hands (therefore, "ifk" means false deities and "khalq" means carving) 1. Then he proceeds to a third reason: that your worship of these idols is either for material benefits or your fate in the other world, and whichever it may be, it is void.

Because "those you worship besides Allah do not possess for you [the power of] provision" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا).

You yourselves admit that the idols are not creators; rather, the Creator is Allah. Therefore, the Provider is also Him. "So seek from Allah provision" (فَابْتَغُوا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الرِّزْقَ).

And since the Provider is Him, "worship Him and be grateful to Him" (وَاعْبُدُوهُ وَاشْكُرُوا لَهُ).

In other words, one of the motives for worship is the sense of gratitude toward the true Benefactor. You know the true Benefactor is Allah, so gratitude and worship are also exclusive to the Pure Essence of Allah.

And if you seek the life of the other abode, know that "to Him you will be returned"—and not to the idols! (إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

The idols are neither a source of effect here nor there.

In this way, with a few short and clear reasons, he crushes their hollow logic.

Then Abraham عليه السلام, as a threat and also out of indifference toward them, says: "And if you deny [me], then nations before you have already denied" (and they were caught in a painful fate) (وَإِنْ تُكَذِّبُوا فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ). "And there is not upon the Messenger except clear notification," whether they accept or not (وَمَا عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلَاغُ الْمُبِينُ).

The meaning of "nations before you" refers to the people of Noah and the nations that came into power after them.

Of course, the connection of the verses necessitates that this sentence be from the words of Abraham عليه السلام, and many commentators have accepted this interpretation or mentioned it as a possibility.

Another possibility is that the address in this verse is to the polytheists of Mecca and the contemporaries of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the phrase "كذّب أمم من قبلكم" (nations before you denied) is more consistent with that. Additionally, similar expressions found in verse 25 of Az-Zumar and verse 25 of Fatir are also regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Arab polytheists. However, in any case, whichever of these two interpretations it may be, there is no difference in terms of the conclusion.

Here, the Quran temporarily leaves the story of Abraham and completes the discussion Abraham had regarding Monotheism and the explanation of his mission by mentioning the proof for the Resurrection (Ma'ad). It says: "Have they not seen how Allah begins creation and then repeats it?" (أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ اللَّهُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ).

The meaning of "seeing" (ru'yah) here is heart-felt observation and knowledge; meaning, do they not know the nature of Divine creation? The same One who had the power for the "first creation" is also capable of repeating it, for power over a thing is power over its likes and similarities.

The possibility also exists that "seeing" here is in the sense of observation with the eye, because man has seen in this world with his own eyes the reviving of dead lands, the growth of plants, the birth of children from sperm, and chicks from eggs. He who has the power for such a task can grant life to the dead after death.

And at the end of the verse, as an emphasis, it adds: "Indeed, that for Allah is easy" (إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ).

Because the renewal of life is considered a simpler matter compared to the creation on the first day.

Of course, this expression is in accordance with human understanding and logic; otherwise, "simple" and "difficult" have no meaning for One whose power is infinite. It is our limited powers that have created these concepts, and with respect to their capability, some matters became difficult and some matters became easy (reflect on this).

Ayahs

قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ ٱلْخَلْقَ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ يُنشِئُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٠)
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how He has originated the creation.’ Then Allah will bring about the genesis of the Hereafter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (20)
يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ (٢١)
He will punish whomever He wishes and have mercy on whomever He wishes, and to Him you will be returned. (21)
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٢٢)
You cannot frustrate Him on the earth or in the sky, nor do you have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (22)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِقَآئِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَئِسُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَتِى وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٣)
Those who deny the signs of Allah and the encounter with Him—they have despaired of My mercy, and for such there is a painful punishment. (23)

Commentary

Those Despairing of Allah's Mercy

These verses continue the discussion on the Resurrection and serve as parenthetical statements in the middle of the story of Ibrahim عليه السلام.

This is not the first time we encounter such a method of discussion; it is the style of the Quran that when the narration of a story reaches a critical stage, it temporarily leaves the sequence to draw necessary conclusions for greater impact.

In any case, the first verse under discussion invites people to an "extrospective journey" (sayr al-afaqi) regarding the issue of Resurrection, whereas the previous verse leaned more toward an "introspective journey" (sayr al-anfusi).

It says: "Say, 'Travel through the land and observe how He began creation'" (قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ بَدَأَ الْخَلْقَ). Observe the various types of living beings, the diverse nations and populations with their unique characteristics, and see how Allah initiated their first creation.

"Then Allah, who has the power to create all these colorful beings and different nations, will produce the final creation [the Hereafter]" (ثُمَّ اللَّهُ يُنْشِئُ النَّشْأَةَ الْآخِرَةَ).

For He has proven His power to everyone through the first creation; indeed, "Allah is over all things Competent" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ). Both this verse and the one preceding it prove the possibility of the Resurrection through the vastness of Allah's power, with the difference being that the first speaks of the initial creation regarding man himself and his surroundings, while the second verse commands the study of the states of other nations and creatures so that people may see the first life in various forms and under completely different conditions, becoming acquainted with the universality of Allah's power and realizing His ability to restore this life.

In truth, just as the proof of Monotheism (Tawhid) is sometimes through observing "introspective signs" and sometimes "extrospective signs," the proof of Resurrection is also established through both paths.

Today, this verse can offer a more precise and deeper meaning to scientists: that they should go and see the remains of the first living beings—found as fossils and otherwise in the depths of the seas, in the heart of mountains, and within the layers of the earth—to discover a corner of the secrets of the beginning of life on Earth and the greatness and power of God, and to know that He is capable of restoring life [1]. Additionally, the word "Nash'ah" originally means to create and nurture something, and sometimes the world is referred to as the "First Creation" (al-nash'ah al-ula) and the Resurrection as the "Final Creation" (al-nash'ah al-akhirah).

It is also noteworthy that at the end of the previous verse, it stated: "Indeed, that is easy for Allah" (إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ), while here it says: "Indeed, Allah is over all things Competent" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ). This difference may be because the first describes a limited study, while the second describes a vast and extensive study.

Then, it mentions one of the issues related to the Resurrection, which is the matter of mercy and punishment, saying: "He punishes whom He wills and has mercy upon whom He wills, and to Him you will be returned" (يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَرْحَمُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ). He punishes those He deems deserving and grants mercy to those He finds worthy.

[1]: Previously, we had a detailed discussion regarding "traveling the earth" and its effects, but that discussion was more focused on learning lessons from the fate of past rebellious nations (refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 102, under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran).

Although Divine mercy precedes His wrath, punishment is mentioned first here because the context is one of warning, and this is appropriate for such a context.

A question arises here: how is it that punishment and mercy are mentioned first, followed by the return of people to Him, when the sequence is actually the opposite—people are first presented before Him and then receive mercy or punishment? Perhaps this is why some consider this punishment and mercy to refer to this world.

In response, we say: based on the context of the preceding and succeeding verses, this punishment and mercy apparently refer to those of the Resurrection, and the phrase "to Him you will be returned" (إِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ) may point to the reason for it; that is, since the return of all of you is to Him and your reckoning is with Him, then punishment and mercy are also in His hand and by His will.

It is also not unlikely that punishment and mercy in this verse have a broad meaning encompassing both this world and the Hereafter.

It is also clear that the phrase "whom He wills" (مَنْ يَشاءُ) refers to a will coupled with wisdom; meaning, whoever He deems worthy and deserving, for Allah's will is not arbitrary but is in harmony with merit and desert.

The word "tuqlabun" is derived from the root "qalb," which originally means to transform something from one state to another. Since in the Resurrection, man is transformed from lifeless dust into a complete living being, this expression is used for his re-creation.

This expression might also hint at the fact that in the abode of the Hereafter, man is so transformed and turned over that his inner self becomes manifest and his internal secrets are revealed, thus evoking the meaning of verse 9 of Surah At-Tariq: "The Day when secrets will be put to trial" (يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ).

To complete the discussion that punishment and mercy are in Allah's hand and the return of all is to Him, it adds: If you imagine you can escape the realm of Allah's sovereignty and that the claws of punishment will not seize you, you are gravely mistaken, for "you cannot escape [Him] on earth or in the heaven" (وَ ما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فِي السَّماءِ) [1]. And if you imagine a guardian or helper will defend you, that too is a pure delusion, because "you have not besides Allah any guardian or any helper" (وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا نَصِيرٍ).

In truth, escaping the grasp of the Lord's punishment is either by leaving the realm of His sovereignty or by staying and defending oneself by relying on the power of others. Neither is leaving possible—for His kingdom is everywhere and the entire universe is His vast property—nor does anyone exist who can stand against His power to defend you.

Two questions remain here:

First: Given that this verse addresses the polytheists and disbelievers who are inhabitants of the earth, what could the expression "nor in the heaven" (وَ لا فِي السَّماءِ) mean?

In response, it must be said: This expression is a type of emphasis and hyperbole; meaning you can neither escape Allah's power within the bounds of the earth, nor in the heavens—for even if you supposedly had the power to go to heaven, you would still be under His power.

Alternatively, it means you cannot frustrate Allah in His will, neither through the people of the earth nor through the deities you imagined for yourselves in heaven, such as angels and jinn (though the first interpretation is more appropriate).

Second: What is the difference between "Wali" (Guardian) and "Nasir" (Helper)?

The late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "A Wali is one who helps a person without being asked, but Nasir is more general; sometimes helping upon request and sometimes without." Rather, it can be said that given the juxtaposition of these two words, Wali refers to a guardian who helps without a request, and Nasir is a rescuer and helper who rushes to a person's aid after a request for help.

In this way, the Quran closes all doors of escape from the grasp of Divine punishment for these criminals.

Therefore, in the next verse, it states decisively: "And those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah and the meeting with Him—those have despaired of My mercy" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ لِقائِهِ أُولئِكَ يَئِسُوا مِنْ رَحْمَتِي). Then, for emphasis, it adds: "and they will have a painful punishment" (وَ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

This "painful punishment" is the necessary consequence of despairing of Allah's mercy.

The "signs of Allah" (ayat Allah) refer either to "cosmic signs"—the traces of Divine greatness in the system of creation—in which case it refers to the issue of Monotheism, while "meeting Him" (liqa'ihi) refers to the issue of Resurrection; meaning they deny both the Origin and the Return.

Or it refers to "legislative signs"—the verses Allah revealed to His prophets which speak of the Origin, Prophethood, and Resurrection—in which case the mention of "meeting Him" is a mention of the specific after the general.

It is also possible that it refers to all of Allah's signs in the worlds of creation and legislation.

It is also necessary to note that "ya'isu" (they despaired) is a past tense verb, even though its primary target is the future, i.e., the Resurrection. This is because it is common in Arabic to describe future events with the past tense when they are 100% certain.

Ayahs

فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ فَأَنجَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٤)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him.’ Then Allah delivered him from the fire. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (24)
وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا مَّوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُم بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٥)
He said, ‘You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of [mutual] affection amongst yourselves in the life of the world. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another, and the Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers.’ (25)
۞ فَـَٔامَنَ لَهُۥ لُوطٌ ۘ وَقَالَ إِنِّى مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٦)
Thereupon Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘Indeed I am migrating toward my Lord. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (26)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ذُرِّيَّتِهِ ٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَجْرَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٢٧)
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained among his descendants prophethood and the Book, and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (27)

Commentary

The Manner of the Arrogant Ones' Response to Ibrahim (عليه السلام)

Now it is time to see what this misguided people said in response to Ibrahim’s (عليه السلام) three arguments regarding Monotheism, Prophethood, and the Resurrection. They certainly had no logical answer, and thus, like all illogical, bullying tyrants, they relied on their satanic power and issued the command for his execution, as the Quran says: "The people of Ibrahim had no answer except that they said: 'Kill him or burn him!'" (فَما كانَ جَوابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا اقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ).

From this expression, it is understood that one group favored burning Ibrahim, while another group suggested his execution by the sword or similar means. Ultimately, the first group prevailed because they believed the worst form of execution was burning with fire.

There is also the possibility that all of them initially thought of executing him by conventional means, but later they all agreed to burn him to exercise the maximum severity.

No mention is made here of the details of Ibrahim's burning; we only read in the continuation of the verse: "Allah delivered him from the fire" (فَأَنْجاهُ اللَّهُ مِنَ النَّارِ).

However, the detailed account of the fire incident appears in Surah Al-Anbiya, verses 68-70, which we have discussed in detail in Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 443 onwards.

At the end, it adds: "Indeed, in this are signs for a people who believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Not just one sign, but several signs exist in this event. For on one hand, the fire's lack of effect on Ibrahim's body was a clear miracle; the turning of the fire into a rose garden—according to the famous account—was another miracle; and the inability of this massive, powerful group against a single individual who was apparently empty-handed of all means is a third miracle.

Furthermore, the lack of impact of this extraordinary, supernatural event on the hearts of those black-hearted people is also a sign of Allah's power, who strips such stubborn individuals of success to the extent that even the greatest signs do not affect them! In a narration, it is stated that when Ibrahim was thrown into the fire with his hands and feet bound, the only thing that burned was the rope with which he was tightly tied [1]. Yes, the fire of ignorance and the crimes of the wicked only burned the means of captivity, and Ibrahim became free! This itself is considered another sign, and perhaps it is for these reasons that in the story of Nuh and his salvation by the ark, He says: "We made it a sign" (in the singular form), while here He says: "signs" (in the plural form).

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 20, p. 130.

In any case, Ibrahim (عليه السلام) was miraculously delivered from that great fire by the grace of the Lord, but he not only did not cease articulating his goals, he gave them even more momentum, speed, and fervor.

"Ibrahim said to them: 'You have taken idols besides Allah only out of mutual affection between yourselves in the life of this world. But know that on the Day of Resurrection, this bond of affection will be completely severed, and each of you will disbelieve in the other, and you will curse one another. Your abode will be the Fire, and you will have no helpers.'" (وَ قالَ إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْثاناً مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ وَ يَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضاً وَ مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ) [1]. How did the choice of idols become a source of affection among the idolaters?

This is a question that can be answered in several ways:

First, idol worship served as a symbol of unity for each tribe and clan, as each group had chosen an idol for themselves. Just as it is written regarding the famous idols of the Arab Jahiliyyah (Age of Ignorance), each belonged to the people of a specific city or tribe (for instance, the idol "Uzza" was specific to the Quraysh, "Lat" belonged to the Thaqif tribe, and "Manat" was specific to the Aws and Khazraj) [2]. Second, the worship of idols created a link between them and their ancestors, and they often used this as an excuse, saying: "These are the relics of our ancestors and we follow them."

[1]: The accusative case of "مودة بينكم" is because it is a "maf'ul lahu" (object of purpose); commentators have offered other possibilities in this regard as well.

[2]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, pp. 86-87.

Beyond this, the leaders of the disbelievers invited their followers to worship idols, and this was a connecting link between the "leaders" and the "followers."

But on the Day of Resurrection, all these hollow, decayed, and flimsy bonds will be severed, and each will cast the blame upon the other, cursing them and disavowing their actions. Even their deities, which in their crude imagination were a means of connecting them to Allah—and about whom they said: "ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفى" ("We do not worship them except that they may bring us closer to Allah in rank") (Surah Az-Zumar, verse 3)—will disavow them.

As the Quran says in Surah Maryam, verse 82: "كَلَّا سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبادَتِهِمْ وَ يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا" ("Nay, they will deny their worship and will be against them!"). Therefore, the meaning of disbelieving in one another and cursing one another is that on that day, they will disavow each other, and what was the source of their false bond of affection in this world will become the source of their enmity and hatred in the Hereafter. As the Quran says in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 67: "الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ":

"Friends on that day will be enemies to one another, except for the righteous!" From some narrations, it is understood that this ruling is not exclusive to idolaters, but rather all those who chose a false leader and followed him, establishing a bond of affection with him, will become enemies of one another on the Day of Resurrection, disavowing and cursing each other [1]. Meanwhile, the bond of affection among the believers, which was formed based on Monotheism, the worship of God, and obedience to the command of Truth in this world, will take on an eternal color and become even stronger there. It is even understood from some narrations that believers will seek forgiveness and intercede for one another there, while the polytheists are busy cursing each other [2].

[1]: Usul al-Kafi—as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 154.

[2]: Kitab al-Tawhid by Saduq, as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 154.

In the next verse, it refers to the faith of Lut and the migration of Ibrahim, saying: "Lut believed in Ibrahim" (فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ). Lut himself was one of the great prophets of Allah and had a close kinship with Ibrahim (it is said he was Ibrahim's nephew). Since the following of a great individual is equivalent to the following of a nation and a people, Allah specifically mentions the faith of Lut—that high-ranking personality contemporary to Ibrahim—so it becomes clear that if others did not believe, it was of no consequence.

Of course, it appears that in the land of Babylon, there were hearts ready to accept Ibrahim's call, and they believed in him after witnessing that great miracle, but they were certainly in the minority.

Then it adds: "Ibrahim said: 'I am migrating to my Lord; indeed, He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise'" (وَ قالَ إِنِّي مُهاجِرٌ إِلى‌ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ). It is clear that when divine leaders have fulfilled their mission in a region, and the environment is so polluted and under the pressure of tyrants that it halts the progress of their call, they must migrate from there to another region to expand the divine message.

Ibrahim (عليه السلام) also moved from the land of Babylon—accompanied by Lut and his wife Sarah—toward the land of Sham (the Levant), the cradle of prophets and Monotheism, so that he could gather strength and followers there and broaden the call of Monotheism.

Interestingly, Ibrahim (عليه السلام) says: "I am migrating to my Lord," because this path was the path of the Lord, the path of His pleasure, and the path of His religion and creed.

Of course, some have suggested that the pronoun in "قال" (he said) refers to Lut, meaning Lut said: "I am migrating to my Lord." The appearance of the sentence is consistent with this meaning, but historical and Quranic evidence shows that the referent of the pronoun is Ibrahim, and Lut's migration was in following Ibrahim.

Evidence for this is verse 99 of Surah As-Saffat, which says on behalf of Ibrahim: "إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى‌ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ": "I am going to my Lord; He will guide me" [1].

[1]: Regarding Ibrahim's migration from the land of Babylon to Sham, a detailed discussion is provided under verse 71 onwards of Surah Al-Anbiya (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 451 onwards).

In the last verse under discussion, mention is made of the four blessings that Allah granted to Ibrahim after this great migration. First was worthy and righteous children—children who could keep the lamp of faith and prophethood burning in his lineage. It says: "We granted him Ishaq and Ya'qub" (وَ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَ يَعْقُوبَ). Two great and worthy prophets, each of whom continued the path and line of Ibrahim (عليه السلام), the breaker of idols.

Second: "In Ibrahim's lineage, We placed prophethood and the Scripture" (وَ جَعَلْنا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِ النُّبُوَّةَ وَ الْكِتابَ). Not only were Ishaq and Ya'qub (his son and grandson) prophets, but the continuation of the line of prophethood in his lineage flowed until the Seal of the Prophets (صلى الله عليه وآله). Prophets arose one after another from this great lineage and illuminated the world with the light of Monotheism.

Third: "We gave him his reward in this world" (وَ آتَيْناهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيا). This reward, which is mentioned in general terms, may refer to various matters: such as a good name and a "truthful tongue" among all nations, for everyone respects Ibrahim (عليه السلام) as a prophet of great stature, takes pride in him, and calls him the "Sheikh of the Prophets." The prosperity of the land of Makkah due to his prayer, the attraction of all hearts toward him, and the remembrance of his magnificent, faith-inspiring, and constructive memories every year during the Hajj rituals are among these rewards.

Fourth: "And indeed, in the Hereafter, he is among the righteous" (وَ إِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ). And this constitutes a complete set of honors.

Key Points

1- The Greatest Honor
