Being included among the righteous, as appears from many verses of the Quran, is the pinnacle of honor that can be bestowed upon a human being. Therefore, many prophets asked Allah to place them among the righteous.

Yusuf, after reaching the highest outward victories, says to the presence of Allah: "تَوَفَّنِي مُسْلِماً وَ أَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ": "Cause me to die a Muslim and join me with the righteous" (Yusuf: 101).

Sulayman also, with all his majesty, status, and glory, says: "أَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ": "O Allah, admit me by Your mercy among Your righteous servants" (An-Naml: 19).

Shu'ayb, that great prophet, when his contract with Musa ends, says: "سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ": "Allah willing, you will find me among the righteous" (Al-Qasas: 27).

Ibrahim also asks for himself to be among the righteous: "رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَ أَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ" (Ash-Shu'ara: 83). And he also asks to have righteous children: "رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ" (As-Saffat: 100).

In many verses, when Allah wishes to praise great prophets, He describes them as being among the righteous. From the sum of these verses, it is well understood that being "righteous" (salih) is the highest stage of a human being's perfection.

What does being righteous mean? It means: merit in terms of belief and faith, merit in terms of action, and merit in terms of speech and ethics. The opposite of righteous (salih) is corrupt (fasid), and we know that "corruption on earth" is an expression that encompasses all injustices, oppressions, and evil deeds. In the Holy Quran, "righteousness" (salah) is sometimes used in contrast to "corruption" (fasad), and sometimes in contrast to "evil" (sayyi'ah), which means sin and bad deeds.

2- Ibrahim's Great Blessings

Some commentators have said that there is a subtle point in the above verse, which is: Allah transformed all of Ibrahim's (عليه السلام) distressing conditions into their opposites:

The idolaters of Babylon wanted to burn Ibrahim with fire; the fire became a rose garden. They wanted him to remain forever alone; Allah granted him such a population and multitude that the world was filled with Ibrahim's lineage. Some of the closest people to him were misguided and idolaters—including Azar—Allah in exchange gave him children who were both guided themselves and became guides for others. Ibrahim (عليه السلام) initially had no wealth or status, but Allah ultimately granted him great wealth and status. Ibrahim (عليه السلام) was initially so unknown that even the idolaters of Babylon, when they wanted to mention him, said: "سَمِعْنا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقالُ لَهُ إِبْراهِيمُ": "We heard a youth speaking of the idols, who is called Ibrahim!" But Allah gave him such a name and reputation that he became known as the "Sheikh of the Prophets" or the "Sheikh of the Messengers" [1].

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, with slight modification.

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you! (28)
أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ ٱلسَّبِيلَ وَتَأْتُونَ فِى نَادِيكُمُ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتِنَا بِعَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٩)
What! Do you come to men, and cut off the way, and commit outrages in your gatherings?’ But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Bring down us Allah’s punishment, if you are truthful.’ (29)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٣٠)
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me against this corruptive lot.’ (30)

Commentary

The Stubborn and Defiled!

After recounting a portion of the story of Ibrahim عليه السلام, the Quran turns to the life of his contemporary Prophet, Lut عليه السلام, and states: "And [remember] Lut, when he said to his people: 'Indeed, you commit an indecency such as no one among the worlds has preceded you in!'" (وَ لُوطاً إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفاحِشَةَ ما سَبَقَكُمْ بِها مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ‌) [1]. The word "Fahishah"—as we have previously mentioned—is derived from the root "Fuhsh," which originally means any extremely ugly and offensive act or speech, and here it is an allusion to "homosexuality."

From the phrase "no one among the worlds has preceded you in it," it is clearly understood that this ugly and shameful act had no precedent, at least not in such a collective, public, and repulsive form, among any previous nation or people.

Regarding the history of the people of Lut, it is written that one of the primary factors for their falling into this sin was that they were a stingy people. Since their cities were located on the trade routes to Syria, they initially performed this act upon some travelers and guests to deter them from staying. However, gradually, homosexual desires grew strong among them, and they sank into the mire of "sodomy."

In any case, they bear both the burden of their own sin and the burden of those who follow their actions in the future (without anything being diminished from the followers' sins), because they were the founders of this ominous and vile tradition. We know that whoever establishes a tradition shares in the deeds of those who act upon it.

Lut عليه السلام, this great Prophet, then expressed his objective more explicitly and said: "Do you indeed approach men [with desire]?" (أَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ‌).

[1]: "Lutan" (Lut) may be conjoined to "Nuhan" (Noah) and thus serves as the object of "Arsalna" (We sent). Some have also taken it as the object of a hidden verb such as "Udhkur" (Remember).

"And do you cut off the way [of human reproduction]?" (وَ تَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ‌) [1] "And do you openly commit evil in your assemblies?" (وَ تَأْتُونَ فِي نادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ).

"Nadi" is derived from "Nida" (call/proclamation), meaning a public assembly, and sometimes it refers to a center of recreation, as people call out to one another there.

The Quran does not detail here what specific evils they committed in their assemblies, but it is self-evident that they were actions consistent with their foul practice. As recorded in some histories, they exchanged vulgar insults and offensive words, slapped one another on the back with their palms, gambled, engaged in childish games—specifically throwing small stones at one another or at passersby—used various musical instruments, and even exposed their bodies and private parts in the presence of the group! [2] In a Hadith from Umm Hani, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, in response to a question about the phrase "and you commit evil in your assemblies," said:

"They used to throw pebbles at whoever passed by them and mock him." [1] Now let us see what the response of this misguided and shameful people was to the logical words of Prophet Lut عليه السلام.

The Quran says: "The answer of his people was only that they said: 'Bring us the punishment of Allah, if you are of the truthful'" (فَما كانَ جَوابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا ائْتِنا بِعَذابِ اللَّهِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌).

Yes, those hedonists who lacked sufficient reason and wisdom spoke these words out of mockery and derision in the face of Lut's reasonable and logical invitation.

From this response, it is clearly understood that Lut, in addition to his reasoned arguments, had also threatened them with painful Divine punishment if they continued their path. However, they ignored everything else and seized upon this one point, and that too out of mockery. A similar point appears in Surah Al-Qamar, verse 36: "And he had already warned them of Our assault, but they disputed the warnings" (وَ لَقَدْ أَنْذَرَهُمْ بَطْشَتَنا فَتَمارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ).

Furthermore, this expression from the misguided people shows that they wanted to conclude from the non-descent of punishment that he was a liar, whereas it is the mercy of Allah that gives even the most defiled nations the opportunity and respite for study, reconsideration, and return.

It was at this point that Lut عليه السلام, finding himself without any other recourse, turned to the threshold of Allah and, with a heart filled with grief and sorrow, "said: 'My Lord, support me against the corrupting people'" (قالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْمُفْسِدِينَ‌).

[1]: A group of commentators have mentioned other possibilities for the phrase "cutting off the way," including: given the history mentioned above, they interpreted it as blocking the path of people traveling to that region, as caravans had no choice but to take detours to avoid falling into the hands of these deviants. Others interpreted it as "stealing people's property." However, the first interpretation mentioned above seems more appropriate, as one of the philosophies for the prohibition of "sodomy," according to explicit narrations, is the danger of cutting off the human lineage.

[2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 517.

A people who have brought corruption and ruin to the earth, cast morality and piety to the wind, abandoned chastity and purity, trampled social justice underfoot, and mixed polytheism and idolatry with moral corruption and oppression, threatening the human race with extinction—O Lord! Grant me victory over these corrupting evildoers.

Key Point: The Plague of Homosexuality!

"Homosexuality," whether between men (sodomy) or between women (lesbianism), is among the worst moral deviations and a source of many corruptions in society.

Fundamentally, the nature of man and woman is created such that they find tranquility and healthy fulfillment in their attraction to the opposite sex (through healthy marriage). Any sexual inclination otherwise is a deviation from healthy human nature and a type of psychological illness that, if continued, intensifies day by day. Its result is a lack of desire for the "opposite sex" and unhealthy fulfillment through the "same sex."

These types of illegitimate relationships have devastating effects on the human organism and even on the nervous system and the soul. It strips a man of being a complete man and a woman of being a complete woman, such that such homosexual men and women suffer from severe sexual weakness and are unable to be good parents for their future children, and sometimes completely lose the power to reproduce.

"Homosexual" individuals gradually turn toward isolation and alienation from society, and then alienation from themselves, becoming trapped in complex psychological conflicts. If they do not seek to reform themselves, they may fall victim to various physical and psychological illnesses.

For this reason, and for other moral and social reasons, Islam has strictly prohibited homosexuality in every shape and form, and has prescribed a severe punishment for it, which sometimes reaches the level of execution.

The important issue is that the permissiveness and morbid pursuit of variety in the materialist civilized world draws many boys and girls toward this great deviation. First, it encourages boys to wear gaudy, feminine clothing and specific self-adornment, and invites girls to wear boyish clothes. From here, the deviation and homosexuality begin, reaching a point where they give legal form to the most shameless acts in this field and consider them exempt from any prosecution or punishment—acts which the pen is ashamed to describe or characterize. [1]

[1]: Regarding the prohibition and the philosophy of the prohibition of homosexuality in Islam, we have had a detailed discussion in Volume 9, page 192 onwards (under verse 81 of Surah Hud).

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوٓا۟ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ۖ إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٣١)
And when Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’ (31)
قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا ۚ قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِيهَا ۖ لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٢)
He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better those who are in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’ (32)
وَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ ۖ إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٣)
And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and in a predicament for their sake. But they said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve! We shall deliver you and your family, except your wife: she will be one of those who remain behind. (33)
إِنَّا مُنزِلُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٣٤)
We are indeed going to bring down upon the people of this town a punishment from the sky because of the transgressions they used to commit.’ (34)
وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَا مِنْهَآ ءَايَةًۢ بَيِّنَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٣٥)
Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for people who exercise their reason. (35)

Commentary

And This is the Fate of the Defiled!

Finally, the prayer of Lut was answered, and the command for a severe and heavy punishment for this transgressing people was issued by the Lord. The angels commissioned with the punishment, before arriving at the land of Lut (peace be upon him) to carry out their mission, went to the land where Ibrahim (peace be upon him) resided to deliver another message: the glad tidings to Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding the birth of children.

The above verses first describe their encounter with Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and state: "When Our messengers came to Ibrahim with the glad tidings (announcing the birth of Ishaq and Ya'qub), they added: 'We are going to destroy the people of this town (referring to the cities of the people of Lut), for its people are wrongdoers'" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا إِبْراهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرى‌ قالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ إِنَّ أَهْلَها كانُوا ظالِمِينَ‌).

The expression "this town" (هذه القرية) indicates that the cities of the people of Lut were in the vicinity of Ibrahim's land.

The term "wrongdoers" (ظالم) is used because they were unjust both to themselves—by choosing the path of polytheism, moral corruption, and indecency—and to others, as their oppression and tyranny even extended to travelers and caravans passing through that land.

When Ibrahim heard this, he became concerned for Lut, the great Prophet of God, and "said: 'But Lut is there!'" (قالَ إِنَّ فِيها لُوطاً). What would be his fate?! But they immediately replied to him: Do not worry, "We know better who is in it" (قالُوا نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِيها).

We never burn the righteous with the wicked; Our plan is perfectly precise and calculated.

They added: "We will certainly save him and his family, except his wife, who will be among those who remain behind" (لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كانَتْ مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ‌).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that in all those cities and towns, there was only one believing and pure family, and Allah rescued them in time, as we read in verse 36 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat: "So We did not find therein any but one house of Muslims" (فَما وَجَدْنا فِيها غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌). Even then, Lut's wife was excluded from the ranks of the believers and was thus condemned to punishment.

The term "ghabirin" (غابرين), the plural of "ghabir," refers to someone who stays behind while their companions depart. A woman who belonged to a prophetic household should not have separated herself from the "Muslims and believers," but her disbelief, polytheism, and idolatry caused her separation.

From this, it becomes clear that her deviation was only in terms of belief. It is not unlikely that she adopted this deviation from her environment, having initially been a believer and monotheist; thus, no criticism is directed at Lut as to why he married such a woman.

Furthermore, if other believers had followed Lut, they would certainly have migrated from that defiled land before this event. It was only Lut and his family who had to remain until the very last hour when there was still a possibility that his preaching and warnings might have an effect.

A question arises here: Did Ibrahim think it possible that divine punishment might also affect Lut, such that he expressed concern to the angels, and they assured him that Lut would be saved?

The clear answer to this question is that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) knew the matter, but he asked for the reassurance of his heart, just as this great Prophet asked regarding the Resurrection, where Allah visualized the scene of the Resurrection for him by reviving birds.

However, the great commentator Allameh Tabataba'i believes that Ibrahim's intention was to use the presence of Lut (peace be upon him) among these people as a reason to avert the punishment from them. He draws support for this from verses 74-76 of Surah Hud, as these verses state: "Ibrahim wanted the punishment of these people to be delayed, perhaps the light of guidance would shine in their hearts, but he was met with the response: 'Do not persist in this matter, for their situation has passed such talk, and their punishment is certain and unchangeable'" [1]. However, in our view, the response the angels gave here regarding the safety of Lut and his family clearly shows that these verses were only discussing Lut himself, whereas the verses in Surah Hud pursue a separate point. And as we said, Ibrahim raised this question only to gain greater reassurance (reflect on this).

The conversation between the angels and Ibrahim ended here, and they set out for the land of Lut (peace be upon him). The Quran says: "When Our messengers came to Lut, he was distressed on their account and felt powerless to protect them" (وَ لَمَّا أَنْ جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا لُوطاً سِي‌ءَ بِهِمْ وَ ضاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعاً).

All his distress stemmed from the fact that he did not recognize them; they appeared as handsome young men, and the arrival of such guests in such a defiled environment could cause trouble for Lut and potentially lead to disgrace in front of the guests. Therefore, he fell into deep thought about what the reaction of the misguided, shameful, and shameless people would be toward these guests. "Si'a" (سيئ) is from the root "sa'a," meaning to fall into a bad state, and "dhar'" (ذرع) means "heart" or character; thus, "daqa bihim dhar'an" (ضاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعاً) means he became narrow-hearted and distressed.

Some commentators have said that this word originally means "the distance between a camel's forelegs while walking," and since a camel shortens and narrows its steps when a heavy load is placed on its back, this phrase is used as a metaphor for a heavy and overwhelming event.

But the guests, perceiving his distress, soon introduced themselves and relieved him of his anxiety: "They said: 'Fear not, nor grieve (these shameless people can do nothing, and soon all will be destroyed). We will save you and your family, except your wife, who will remain among them'" (and perish) (وَ قالُوا لا تَخَفْ وَ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَ أَهْلَكَ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ كانَتْ مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ‌).

Of course, it is clearly understood from the verses of Surah Hud that when those shameless people learned of the presence of Lut's guests, they rushed to him intending to harass them. Lut, who had not yet recognized the angels, was deeply distressed. Sometimes he resorted to advice, sometimes to threats, and sometimes by appealing to their conscience—asking if there was not one right-minded man among them—and sometimes by offering his daughters in marriage to them, trying to deter them. But these shameless people were satisfied with nothing and thought only of their disgraceful goal! However, the messengers of the Lord introduced themselves to Lut (peace be upon him) and, through a divine miracle, blinded the eyes of the attacking mob, pouring cold water on the burning heart of this great Prophet [1]. It is noteworthy that the messengers of the Lord said to Lut: "Fear not" and "Grieve not." Regarding the difference between these two words (khawf and huzn), Al-Mizan states that "khawf" (fear) is regarding potential future unpleasant events, while "huzn" (grief) is regarding certain ones.

Some consider "khawf" to relate to future events and "gham" (sorrow) to past events.

It is also possible that "khawf" is used for "dangerous" matters, while grief relates to "painful" events, even if there is no danger in them.

A question arises here: according to the verses of Surah Hud, Lut's fear and grief were not for himself, but rather he feared they would harass his guests. Yet the answer the angels gave related to the salvation of Lut and his family, and these two do not seem to align.

The answer to this question can be inferred generally from verse 81 of Surah Hud, for when the shameless people came to lay hands on the guests, the angels said to Lut: "These people shall not reach you," meaning if they cannot reach us, they certainly cannot harm you either. Therefore, the angels took their own safety for granted—and indeed it was—and focused the glad tidings solely on the salvation of Lut and his family.

Then, to make the fate of this disgrace-polluted group clearer in their mission plan, they added: "We are about to bring down upon the people of this town a punishment from heaven because of their rebellion and sin!" (إِنَّا مُنْزِلُونَ عَلى‌ أَهْلِ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزاً مِنَ السَّماءِ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ‌). The "town" refers to the city of "Sodom" and the surrounding cities and towns where the people of Lut lived; some have estimated their population at seven hundred thousand [1]. "Rijz" (رجز) here means "punishment" (the original meaning of "rijz" is agitation or trembling; subsequently, anything that causes agitation is called rijz, and thus the Arabs use it for many meanings such as severe calamities, plague, heavy snow and hail, idols, the whispers of Satan, and divine punishment).

The phrase "because of their rebellion" (بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ‌) explains the reason for their painful punishment, which was their transgression and disobedience to Allah. The use of the present continuous verb "yafsiqun" indicates the persistence and continuation of their evil deeds.

This expression reflects the reality that if they had refrained from continuing their sin and returned to the path of truth, piety, and purity, they would not have been afflicted with such punishment, and their past would have been forgiven.

The nature of their painful punishment is not explained here; it only says: "And We have left thereof (from those towns, the crumbled ruins and the stricken, destroyed cities) a clear sign for a people who understand" (وَ لَقَدْ تَرَكْنا مِنْها آيَةً بَيِّنَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ‌). However, in Surah Hud, verse 82, and also Surah Al-A'raf, verse 84, the details of their punishment are given: first, a severe earthquake completely overturned their cities, and then a rain of stones from the sky fell upon them, such that their bodies and the ruins of their houses and palaces were buried beneath it.

The expression "a clear sign" (آية بينة) refers to the remaining traces of the city of "Sodom," which, according to Quranic verses, was located on the caravan route of the people of Hijaz and remained until the time of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), as we read in verse 76 of Surah Al-Hijr: "And surely it is on a path that still exists" (وَ إِنَّها لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُقِيمٍ‌). And in verses 137 and 138 of As-Saffat, it is stated: "And surely you pass by them in the morning and at night; will you not then understand?" (وَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَ بِاللَّيْلِ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 129. [^1]: The details of this event were explained in Vol. 9, p. 178 onwards, under verses 77 to 81 of Surah Hud. [^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 6, p. 467.

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱرْجُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٣٦)
And to Midian We sent Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah, and expect [to encounter] the Last Day, and do not act wickedly on the earth causing corruption.’ (36)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٣٧)
But they impugned him, whereupon the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (37)
وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَا۟ وَقَد تَّبَيَّنَ لَكُم مِّن مَّسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۖ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ (٣٨)
And ‘Ad and Thamud, [whose fate] is evident to you from their habitations. Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, thus he barred them from the way [of Allah], though they used to be perceptive. (38)
وَقَـٰرُونَ وَفِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ سَـٰبِقِينَ (٣٩)
And Korah, Pharaoh, and Haman. Certainly, Moses brought them manifest proofs, but they acted arrogantly in the land; though they could not outmaneuver [Allah]. (39)
فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنَا بِذَنۢبِهِۦ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِ حَاصِبًا وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ خَسَفْنَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَغْرَقْنَا ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٠)
So We seized each [of them] for his sin: among them were those upon whom We unleashed a rain of stones, and among them were those who were seized by the Cry, and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow, and among them were those whom We drowned. It was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (40)

Commentary

Every Oppressive Group Was Punished in a Specific Way

After the story of Lot and his people, it is the turn of other nations such as the "People of Shu'ayb," "Ad," "Thamud," "Qarun," and "Pharaoh," each of whom is mentioned briefly and concisely in the verses under discussion to reach a general conclusion.

First, It says: "And to Midian [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb" (وَ إِلى‌ مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً) [1]. The expression "brother," as we have mentioned many times, refers to the ultimate affection of these prophets toward their nations and their lack of desire for dominance; of course, these prophets often had kinship ties with their peoples as well.

"Midian" is a city in southwestern Jordan, known today as "Ma'an," located east of the "Gulf of Aqaba," where Prophet Shu'ayb and his people lived. [1] Shu'ayb, like other great prophets of God, began his call with belief in the Origin (Allah) and the Resurrection, which are the foundation and basis of every religion and creed. "He said: 'O my people! Worship Allah and hope for the Last Day'" (فَقالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ ارْجُوا الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ).

Faith in the Origin causes a person to feel a constant, precise supervision from the Lord over his actions, and faith in the Resurrection reminds man of the Great Court where everything will be examined without the slightest omission. Belief in these two principles will certainly have an extraordinary impact on the education and reform of a human being.

Shu'ayb's third command was a comprehensive practical instruction covering all social programs; he said: "And do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption" (وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ‌).

Corruption (Fasad) has a broad meaning that encompasses any kind of disorder, destruction, deviation, and oppression, and its opposite is "Salah" (righteousness) and reform, which includes all constructive programs within its concept.

"Ta'thaw" is derived from the root "Athaya," meaning to produce corruption; however, this expression is mostly used regarding moral corruption. Therefore, mentioning the word "mufsideen" (corruptors) after it serves as an emphasis.

[^1]: This sentence is conjoined to the sentence "And We certainly sent Noah" and what follows it. [^1]: Regarding "Midian," we had a detailed explanation under verse 23 of Surah Al-Qasas in this same volume of Tafsir Nemouneh.

But that group, instead of listening to the advice of this great reformer with the ear of their soul, rose in opposition and "denied him" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ‌). "This act caused a severe earthquake to seize them!" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ).

"And as a result of this incident, they lay prostrate in their homes, dead!" (فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

"Jathim" is from the root "Jathama," meaning to sit on one's knees and to remain in one place. It is not unlikely that this expression refers to the fact that they were asleep at the time of this severe earthquake; they suddenly rose, and as soon as they sat on their knees, the incident gave them no respite, and with the collapsing of walls and the lightning bolt that accompanied that deadly earthquake, they lost their lives. [1]

The next verse speaks of the people of "Ad" and "Thamud," without mentioning their prophets (Hud and Salih) or their dialogues with these two rebellious nations, because they were well-known nations and the stories of their prophets have appeared repeatedly in other verses of the Quran. It says: "And [We destroyed] Ad and Thamud" (وَ عاداً وَ ثَمُودَ) [2].

[^1]: The description of the painful story of the people of "Shu'ayb" is found under verses 84 to 95 of Surah Hud (Volume 9, pages 199 to 221). [^2]: "Ad" and "Thamud" are objects of the verb "ahlakna" (We destroyed) which is implied and understood from the previous verse; some have considered them objects of "udhkur" (remember).

Then it adds: "And their dwellings have become apparent to you" (and the ruins of their cities in the lands of Hijr and Yemen are on your path) (وَ قَدْ تَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ مِنْ مَساكِنِهِمْ‌).

Every year during your trade journeys to Yemen and Sham, you pass through the land of "Hijr" in the north of the Arabian Peninsula and "Ahqaf" in the south near Yemen, and you see the ruins of the cities of Ad and Thamud with your own eyes; why do you not take heed?! Then it points to the main cause of their misery, saying: "Satan made their deeds seem fair to them, and thus diverted them from the [right] way" (وَ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

"While they were endowed with insight [and intellect]," their nature (fitrah) was based on monotheism and piety, and the divine prophets had sufficiently shown them the way (وَ كَانُوا مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ‌).

Some commentators have interpreted this phrase as having seeing eyes and sufficient intellect and understanding, some as possessing a sound nature, and some as having access to the guidance of the prophets.

There is no obstacle to all of these being combined in the meaning of the verse, indicating that they were not "ignorant out of incapacity" (jahil-e qasir); rather, they knew the truth well beforehand, had an awake conscience, sufficient intellect, and the prophets had completed the proof (itmam al-hujjah) for them. Yet, despite all these calls of intellect and conscience, they abandoned the call of the prophets and followed Satanic whispers. Day by day, their ugly and ominous deeds appeared more beautiful in their eyes, until they reached a point where there was no way back. The law of creation set fire to these dry and fruitless logs, for "this is the very desert of one who rejects the Educator!"

The next verse names three of the tyrants, each of whom was a prominent example of a Satanic power, saying: "And [We destroyed] Qarun, Pharaoh, and Haman" (وَ قارُونَ وَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ هامانَ‌) [1]. Qarun was the manifestation of wealth coupled with pride, selfishness, and negligence; Pharaoh was the manifestation of arrogant power coupled with diabolism; and Haman was a model for assisting arrogant oppressors.

Then it adds: "Moses came to these three with clear signs and completed the proof for them" (وَ لَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مُوسى‌ بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌).

"But they took the path of arrogance, pride, and rebellion on the earth" (فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

Qarun relied on his wealth, adornments, treasures, and knowledge, while Pharaoh and Haman relied on the army, military power, and the massive propaganda force among the unaware masses.

But "despite all this, they could not outstrip Allah or escape from the grasp of His power" (وَ ما كانُوا سابِقِينَ‌).

Allah gave the command for the destruction of Qarun to the earth, which was his cradle of comfort, and the command for the destruction of Pharaoh and Haman to the water, which is the source of life. To destroy them, God did not mobilize the armies of heaven and earth; rather, that which was the source of their life executed the command for their death! [2] "Sabiqeen" is the plural of "sabiq," meaning one who outstrips and gets ahead. When He says: "they did not outstrip," it means they could not flee from the realm of God's power with the resources they had at their disposal, nor escape divine punishment; rather, at the very moment Allah willed, He sent them to the land of non-existence with humiliation and abasement.

[^1]: These three words are also objects of an implied verb (ahlakna) understood from the previous verses; some consider them objects of "udhkur." [^2]: The description of the life story of Qarun is mentioned in seven verses of Surah Al-Qasas (76-81), and the destruction of Pharaoh and his people is mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Qasas in this same volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, and in Volume 6 in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf.

As the next verse states: "So each We seized for his sin" (فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنا بِذَنْبِهِ‌). And since four groups were actually mentioned in the two previous verses whose punishments were not stated ("the people of Ad and Thamud," "Qarun," and "Pharaoh and Haman"), the verse follows by stating their punishments in order:

"Upon some of them We sent a violent storm of stones" (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِ حاصِباً).

"Hasib" refers to a storm in which pebbles and stones are set in motion ("hasba" means pebble). This group refers to the people of Ad, over whom—according to Surahs Adh-Dhariyat, Al-Haqqah, and Al-Qamar—a severe and crushing storm prevailed for seven nights and eight days, smashing their houses and scattering their corpses like autumn leaves (Surah Al-Haqqah, verses 5 to 7).

"And some were seized by the [heavenly] blast" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَخَذَتْهُ الصَّيْحَةُ).

We have said: the heavenly blast is the result of lightning bolts accompanied by an earthquake at its center, and this was the punishment descended upon the people of Thamud and some other nations, as stated in Surah Hud, verse 67 regarding the people of Thamud: "And the blast seized those who did wrong, and they lay prostrate in their homes."

"And some of them We caused the earth to swallow" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ خَسَفْنا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ‌).

This was the punishment realized in the case of Qarun, the proud and arrogant wealthy man of the Israelites, which is referred to in verse 81 of Surah Al-Qasas.

"And finally, some of them We drowned" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَغْرَقْنا).

We know this refers to Pharaoh, Haman, and their followers, which has been discussed in various surahs of the Quran.

In any case, according to this explanation, the four aforementioned punishments are respectively for the four groups whose deviation, misguidance, and sin were mentioned in the two previous verses without their punishments being stated.

As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that these punishments might include other nations as well (such as drowning for the people of Noah and the rain of stones for the people of Lot), it seems very unlikely. This is because their punishments were stated where the Quran described their circumstances, and there was no need for repetition. What had not been stated in this series of verses was the punishment of the four groups mentioned in the last two verses.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize the reality that all of these were caught by the reaction to their own deeds and were reaping what they themselves had sown, it says: "Allah would never have wronged them, but it was they who were wronging themselves" (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ‌).

Yes, the punishments of this world and the next are reflections and embodiments of human actions, occurring when they close all paths of reform and return upon themselves. Allah is too Just to permit the slightest oppression or wrong against a human being.

This verse, like many other verses of the Quran, clearly establishes the principle of human free will and choice, clarifying the truth that decisions everywhere belong to the human being himself. God created him free and willed him to be free. Therefore, the belief of the followers of the school of determinism (Jabr), which unfortunately exists among some Muslims as well, is invalidated by this powerful logic of the Quran.

Ayahs

مَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْلِيَآءَ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْعَنكَبُوتِ ٱتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتًا ۖ وَإِنَّ أَوْهَنَ ٱلْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ ٱلْعَنكَبُوتِ ۖ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
The parable of those who take guardians instead of Allah is that of the spider that takes a home, and indeed the frailest of homes is the home of a spider, had they known! (41)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٤٢)
Allah indeed knows whatever thing they invoke besides Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (42)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ ۖ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْعَـٰلِمُونَ (٤٣)
We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who have knowledge. (43)
خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٤)
Allah created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. There is indeed a sign in that for the faithful. (44)

Commentary

Fragile Supports Like a Spider’s Web!

In the preceding verses, the painful and tragic fate of the corrupt polytheists, the stubborn arrogant, and the tyrannical, selfish oppressors was described. Correspondingly, the verses under discussion present a striking and eloquent parable for those who take others besides Allah as their deities and guardians—a parable that yields more insights the more one contemplates it.

He says: "The parable of those who take protectors and deities besides Allah is that of the spider who builds a house; and indeed, the flimsiest of houses is the house of the spider, if they only knew!" (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْلِياءَ كَمَثَلِ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ اتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتاً وَ إِنَّ أَوْهَنَ الْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

What an expressive and beautiful example! What an eloquent and precise metaphor! Observe closely: every animal and insect has a home or a nest, but none of these dwellings is as fragile as the spider's web.

Fundamentally, a house should have walls, a roof, and a door, protecting its inhabitant from incidents and securing its prey, food, and needs within. Some houses lack a roof but at least have walls, or if they lack walls, they have a roof.

However, the spider's nest, constructed from a few extremely thin threads, has neither walls, nor a roof, nor a courtyard, nor a door—all this on one hand.

On the other hand, its materials are so weak and non-durable that they cannot withstand any incident.

If a gentle breeze blows, it unravels its warp and woof!

If a few drops of rain fall upon it, it disintegrates! The slightest flame of fire reaching it destroys it.

Even if dust settles upon it, it tears apart and hangs from the ceiling.

The false deities of this group likewise possess neither benefit nor harm; they solve no problems and serve as no sanctuary for anyone on a day of desperation.

It is true that for the spider, with its long and extended legs, this house is both a center for rest and a "shop" and trap for catching insects and obtaining food.

Yet, compared to the houses of other animals and insects, it is infinitely weak and non-durable.

Those who rely on other than Allah as their support are leaning on a spider's web.

Those who choose deities besides Allah are leaning on a spider's web. The thrones and crowns of the Pharaohs, the countless wealth of the Qaruns, the palaces and treasures of kings—all are like spider webs.

Non-durable, weak, unreliable, and unstable in the face of the storms of events.

History also demonstrates that truly none of these things can serve as a support for man.

But those who rely on faith and trust in Allah lean upon a steel dam.

It is also necessary to mention this point here: although the spider's house and its threads are a proverb for fragility, they are themselves among the wonders of creation. Contemplating them makes man more acquainted with the greatness of the Creator.

Spider silk is made from a viscous liquid located in tiny pores, like the head of a needle, under its abdomen. This liquid has a specific composition that hardens and becomes strong whenever it is exposed to air.

The spider draws it out of these pores using its special claws and constructs its webs from it.

It is said that each spider is capable of spinning about five hundred meters of these threads with the very limited liquid it has at its disposal! Some have written that the fragility of these threads is due to their extreme thinness; otherwise, they are stronger than a steel wire of the same thickness!

Remarkably, these threads are sometimes each composed of four strands, and each strand itself is composed of a thousand filaments! Each emerges from a tiny hole in its body. Now consider how delicate, precise, and thin each of these secondary filaments is produced.

In addition to the wonders used in the building materials of the spider's house, its structural form and engineering are also fascinating. If we look closely at intact spider webs, we see a beautiful sight like a sun with its rays set upon specific foundations made of these same threads. Of course, this house is a suitable and ideal home for the spider, but overall, nothing flimsier can be imagined—and such are the deities worshipped besides Allah.

Considering that there is not just one type of spider—rather, some scientists claim that twenty thousand species of spiders have been identified so far!—and each has its own characteristics, the greatness of Allah's power in creating this small creature becomes even more apparent.

Furthermore, the use of the term "Awliya" (plural of Wali/protector) instead of "Asnam" (idols) is perhaps to point out that not only man-made deities but also non-divine leaders and guides fall under this same ruling.

The phrase "if they only knew" (لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ) at the end of the verse refers to the idols and false deities, not to the fragility of the spider's web, for everyone knows its fragility. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence is: if they were aware of the weakness of the deities and foundations they have chosen besides Allah, they would have well known that these are as fragile as a spider's web.

In the next verse, He gives a threatening warning to these heedless and uninformed polytheists, saying: "Indeed, Allah knows whatever thing they call upon besides Him" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ). Their overt polytheism and their hidden, secret polytheism—none of it is concealed from Allah.

"And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

If He grants them respite, it is not because He does not know or because His power is limited; rather, His wisdom necessitates giving sufficient opportunity so that the proof is completed for all, and those worthy of guidance may be guided.

Some commentators have considered this sentence an allusion to the excuses the polytheists fabricated for themselves: that if we worship these idols, it is not for their own sake; they are in fact manifestations and symbols of the stars of heaven, or of prophets and angels. We actually prostrate to them and honor them, and our good and evil, our profit and loss, are in their hands.

The Quran says: Allah knows what things you call upon; whoever they may be and whatever they may be, they are like a spider's web before the power of His command and have nothing of their own to give you.

The third verse under discussion seemingly refers to an objection that the enemies of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) raised against these parables, saying: How is it possible for the God who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth to strike parables of spiders, flies, insects, and the like? The Quran replies to them: "And these parables We strike for mankind, and none understands them except the learned" (وَ تِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَ ما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ).

The importance and subtlety of a parable do not lie in its greatness or smallness, but in its correspondence to the intended meaning. Sometimes, its smallness is its greatest point of strength.

For instance, when speaking of fragile and baseless supports, the example must be chosen from the spider's web, which can reflect this fragility, instability, and lack of permanence better than anything else. This is the essence of eloquence and clarity.

It is here that He says only the learned perceive the intricacies of the Quranic parables.

In the final verse under discussion, He adds: "Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth; indeed, in that is a great sign for the believers" (خَلَقَ اللَّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ). Falsehood and futility find no way into His work. If He strikes a parable of a spider and its fragile, foundationless house, it is based on a calculation; and if He has chosen a small creature for an illustration, it is to express the truth. Otherwise, He is the Creator of the largest galaxies and celestial systems.

It is interesting that at the end of these few verses, the emphasis is on "knowledge" and "faith." In one place He says: "if they only knew" (لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ); in another place He says: "none understands them except the learned" (وَ ما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ).

And here He says: "indeed, in that is a great sign for the believers" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

This indicates that the face of truth is bright and sunny, but it blossoms in fertile grounds. An aware and seeking heart, an awakened soul, and submission to the truth are necessary. If these blind-hearted ones do not see the beauty of the truth, it is not because of its concealment, but because of their own blindness.

Ayah

ٱتْلُ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ ۗ وَلَذِكْرُ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَصْنَعُونَ (٤٥)
Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book, and maintain the prayer. Indeed the prayer restrains from indecent and wrongful conduct, and the remembrance of Allah is surely greater. And Allah knows whatever [deeds] you do. (45)

Commentary

Prayer as a Deterrent Against Evil and Vice

After concluding various sections regarding the history of past nations and great prophets, their unfavorable treatment of these divine leaders, and the tragic end of their lives, the discourse turns toward the Prophet—for the sake of comfort, consolation, strengthening of morale, and providing a general and comprehensive roadmap—giving him two commands:

First, It says: "Recite what has been revealed to you of the Book (the Quran)" (اتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ‌).

Read these verses, for everything you desire is within them: knowledge and wisdom, advice and exhortation, the criteria for distinguishing truth from falsehood, the means for the illumination of the heart and soul, and the path of movement for every group and society.

Read and apply it in your life; read and take inspiration from it; read and enlighten your heart with the light of its recitation.

After stating this command, which in reality has an educational aspect, He proceeds to the second command, which is the primary branch of spiritual cultivation, saying: "And establish prayer" (وَ أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ).

Then, He addresses the great philosophy of prayer, saying: "Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing" (إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى‌ عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَ الْمُنْكَرِ) [^1]. The nature of prayer, since it reminds man of the most powerful deterrent factor—namely, belief in the Origin and the Resurrection—possesses the effect of deterring one from indecency and evil.

A person who stands for prayer, says Takbir, considers Allah greater and higher than everything, remembers His blessings, offers Him praise and thanks, extols Him for His Mercy and Compassion, remembers His Day of Judgment, confesses to being His servant, seeks help from Him, asks Him for the Straight Path, and seeks refuge in Allah from the path of those who have earned His anger and those who are astray (the essence of Surah al-Hamd).

Undoubtedly, in the heart and soul of such a person, a movement toward the Truth, a motion toward purity, and a leap toward piety are born.

He performs Ruku’ (bowing) for Allah, places his forehead on the dust in His presence, becomes immersed in His greatness, and forgets selfishness and superiority complexes.

He bears witness to His Oneness and testifies to the messengership of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

He sends salutations upon his Prophet and raises his hands toward the threshold of God to be placed among His righteous servants (Tashahhud and Salam).

All these matters create a wave of spirituality within him, a wave that is considered a powerful barrier against sin.

This act is repeated several times throughout the day and night; when he rises in the morning, he is immersed in His remembrance.

In the middle of the day, when he is submerged in material life, he suddenly hears the Takbir of the Mu'adhin, interrupts his schedule, hastens to His threshold, and even at the end of the day and the beginning of the night, before going to his bed of rest, he engages in intimate supplication with Him and makes his heart a center for His lights.

Furthermore, when he prepares the preliminaries for prayer, he washes and purifies himself, distances himself from what is forbidden (Haram) and usurped, and goes to the court of the Friend; all these matters have a deterrent effect against the path of immorality and wrongdoing.

However, every prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing to the same extent that it possesses the conditions of perfection and the spirit of worship—sometimes a comprehensive and total prohibition, and sometimes a partial and limited one.

It is impossible for someone to pray and for it to have no effect on him, even if his prayer is merely formal, and even if he is tainted by sin. Of course, the effect of such a prayer is small; if such individuals did not perform even that prayer, they would be even more tainted.

To put it more clearly: the prohibition of immorality and wrongdoing has many levels and degrees, and every prayer, in proportion to the observance of its conditions, possesses some of these degrees.

From what we have said above, it becomes clear that the confusion of a group of commentators in interpreting this verse and choosing inappropriate interpretations is groundless. Perhaps because they saw that some people pray yet commit sins, and they viewed the verse in its absolute sense without considering the levels of hierarchy, they fell into doubt and chose other paths in interpreting the verse.

Among these, some have said: Prayer keeps man from immorality and wrongdoing as long as he is engaged in the prayer!! What a strange statement! This is not a merit for prayer; many actions are like this.

Others have said that the acts and litanies of prayer are like sentences, each of which forbids man from immorality and wrongdoing; for example, Takbir, Tasbih, and Tahlil each tell the person "do not sin." Whether the person listens to this prohibition or not is another matter.

But those who interpreted the above verse in this way were heedless of the fact that the prohibition here is not just a "legislative prohibition" (nahy tashri'i), but rather a "generative prohibition" (nahy takwini). The literal meaning of the verse is that prayer has a deterrent effect, and the primary interpretation is what we stated above. Of course, there is no obstacle to saying that prayer performs both a generative and a legislative prohibition against immorality and wrongdoing.

Note several Hadiths: 1- In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Whoever's prayer does not prohibit him from immorality and wrongdoing, it only increases his distance from Allah!" [^1]

2- In another Hadith from the same Prophet, it is stated: "There is no prayer for one who does not obey the prayer, and obedience to the prayer is to refrain from immorality and wrongdoing." [^2]

[^1]: The difference between "Fahsha" (immorality) and "Munkar" (wrongdoing) is mentioned in Volume 11, page 370, under verse 90 of Surah an-Nahl. In a short phrase, it can be said: "Fahsha" refers to major hidden sins, and "Munkar" refers to major manifest sins; or "Fahsha" are sins committed due to the dominance of the power of lust, and "Munkar" due to the dominance of the power of anger. [^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed (the latter Hadith suggests a legislative prohibition).

3- In a third Hadith from that same noble figure, we read: A young man from the Ansar used to perform the prayer with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, yet he was tainted with ugly sins. This situation was reported to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and he said: "Eventually, his prayer will one day prohibit him from these acts." [^1]

4- This effect of prayer is so important that in some Islamic narrations, it is mentioned as the criterion for measuring an accepted versus an unaccepted prayer. As Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Whoever wishes to know whether his prayer has been accepted or not, let him look: did his prayer prevent him from immorality and wrongdoing? To the extent that it prevented him, it has been accepted from him!" [^2]

In the continuation of the verse, He adds: "And the remembrance of Allah is even greater" (وَ لَذِكْرُ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ). The apparent meaning of the above sentence is the statement of a more important philosophy for prayer; that is, another of the important effects and blessings of prayer, which is even more important than the prohibition of immorality and wrongdoing, is that it reminds man of Allah, which is the root and primary source of every good and happiness. Indeed, the primary factor in the prohibition of immorality and wrongdoing is this very "remembrance of Allah" (dhikrullah). In fact, its superiority is because it is considered the cause and the root.

Fundamentally, the remembrance of Allah is the source of life for hearts and tranquility for souls, and nothing reaches its level: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest" (Surah ar-Ra'd, verse 28).

[^1] and [^2]: Same source.

Fundamentally, the spirit of all acts of worship—whether prayer or otherwise—is the remembrance of Allah. The words of the prayer, the actions of the prayer, the preliminaries of the prayer, and the post-prayer supplications, all of them in reality revive the remembrance of Allah in the heart of man.

It is noteworthy that in verse 14 of Surah Taha, this fundamental philosophy of prayer is mentioned, addressing Moses: "Establish prayer for My remembrance" (أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي‌).

However, great commentators have mentioned other interpretations for the above sentence, and some Islamic narrations also hint at these interpretations, including:

The meaning of the above sentence is that "Allah's remembrance of you through mercy" is greater than "your remembrance of Him through obedience." [^1] Another is that the remembrance of Allah is greater and higher than the prayer itself, because the spirit of every worship is the remembrance of Allah.

These interpretations, some of which have appeared in Islamic narrations, might be references to the inner depths (batn) of the verse; otherwise, its literal meaning is more consistent with the first meaning. In most cases where dhikrullah is used, the meaning is people's remembrance of Allah, and the above verse also evokes this meaning. However, of course, Allah's remembrance of His servants can be a direct result of the servants' remembrance of Allah, and in this way, the conflict between the two meanings is resolved.

In a Hadith from Mu'adh ibn Jabal, it is stated: "None of the deeds of man is superior for his salvation from divine punishment than the remembrance of Allah." They asked him, "Even Jihad in the way of Allah?" He said, "Yes, for Allah says: 'And the remembrance of Allah is greater.'"

[^1]: According to this interpretation, "Allah" here is the "subject" (fa'il), but according to the previous interpretation, it is the "object" (maf'ul) for the mentioned verb.

It appears that Mu'adh ibn Jabal derived this statement from the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, for he himself narrates that he asked the Messenger of Allah: "Which deed is the most superior of all deeds?" He replied: "That you die while your tongue is moist with the remembrance of Allah, the Almighty and Majestic."

And since the intentions of humans and the level of their presence of heart in prayer and other acts of worship vary greatly, at the end of the verse He says: "And Allah knows what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَصْنَعُونَ‌). Whether the deeds you perform in secret or openly, the intentions you hold in your heart, or the words you utter with your tongue.

Key Points The Impact of Prayer on the Education of the Individual and Society

Although prayer is not something whose philosophy is hidden from anyone, careful study of the texts of the verses and Islamic narrations guides us to more subtle details in this regard:

1- The spirit, foundation, goal, basis, preliminary, result, and ultimately the philosophy of prayer is the remembrance of Allah; it is that very "remembrance of Allah" which is stated in the above verse as the supreme result.

Of course, it is a remembrance that is a preliminary to thought, and a thought that is the motive for action, as stated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who, in interpreting the sentence "And the remembrance of Allah is greater," said: "Remembering Allah regarding what He has made lawful and what He has prohibited." (Meaning he remembers Allah and goes toward the lawful and turns away from the prohibited) [^1]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 82, p. 200.

2- Prayer is a means of washing away sins and seeking divine forgiveness, because prayer inevitably invites man to repentance and the rectification of the past. Thus, we read in a Hadith: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked his companions: "If there were a river at the door of one of your houses and he bathed in it five times every day, would any dirt remain on his body?" I said, "No." He said, "Indeed, the likeness of prayer is like that of a flowing river; whenever one prays, the sins committed between them are expiated." [^1] In this way, the wounds inflicted upon the soul and spirit of man by sin are healed with the ointment of prayer, and the rust that settles on the heart is scrubbed away.

3- Prayer is a barrier against future sins, because it strengthens the spirit of faith in man and nurtures the seedling of piety in the heart. We know that "faith" and "piety" are the strongest barriers against sin, and this is exactly what is stated in the above verse as the prohibition of immorality and wrongdoing. It is also what we read in several Hadiths: there were sinful individuals whose situation was described to the leaders of Islam, and they said: "Do not grieve, their prayer will rectify them," and it did.

4- Prayer dispels heedlessness. The greatest tragedy for the wayfarers of the path of Truth is to forget the purpose of their creation and become submerged in material life and fleeting pleasures.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 3, p. 7 (Chapter 2 of the Chapters on the Number of Obligatory Prayers, Hadith 3).

However, prayer, by virtue of being performed at different intervals five times a day and night, constantly warns man, alerts him, reminds him of the purpose of his creation, and points out his position in the universe. This is a great blessing that man has a means at his disposal to give him a powerful wake-up call several times a day.

5- Prayer breaks down self-centeredness and pride, because man places his forehead on the dust before Allah seventeen units (rak'ahs) a day and night, twice in each unit. He sees himself as a tiny atom before His greatness—rather, a zero before infinity.

It pulls back the curtains of vanity and selfishness and crushes arrogance and superiority-seeking.

For this reason, Ali عليه السلام, in that famous Hadith where the philosophies of Islamic worship are reflected, explains the first worship after faith—which is prayer—with this very goal, saying: "Allah mandated faith as a purification from polytheism, and prayer as a sanctification from pride..." [^1]

6- Prayer is a means of nurturing moral virtues and the spiritual perfection of man, because it takes man out of the limited world of matter and the four walls of the natural realm, invites him to the kingdom of the heavens, and makes him a companion and confidant of the angels. He sees himself before Allah without the need for any intermediary and begins a dialogue with Him.

The repetition of this act throughout the day and night, while emphasizing the attributes of Allah—His Mercy, Compassion, and Greatness—especially with the help of various Surahs of the Quran after al-Hamd, which are the best inviters toward goodness and purity, has a significant effect on nurturing moral virtues within man.

Thus, we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding the philosophy of prayer: "Prayer is the means of nearness to Allah for every pious person." [^1]

7- Prayer gives value and spirit to man's other deeds—because prayer revives the spirit of sincerity, as prayer is a collection of pure intention, clean speech, and sincere actions. The repetition of this collection throughout the day and night sows the seeds of other good deeds in the soul of man and strengthens the spirit of sincerity.

Thus, we read in a famous Hadith that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in his final testament after his blessed head was split by the sword of the criminal Ibn Muljam, said: "Fear Allah, fear Allah regarding the prayer, for it is the pillar of your religion." [^2] We know that when the pillar of a tent breaks or falls, no matter how strong the surrounding ropes and pegs may be, they are of no use. Similarly, when the connection of the servants with Allah through prayer is severed, other deeds will lose their effect.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The first thing for which a servant will be held accountable is the prayer; if it is accepted, his other deeds will be accepted, and if it is rejected, his other deeds will be rejected!" Perhaps the reason for this statement is that prayer is the symbol of the connection between the creation and the Creator. If it is performed correctly, the intention of seeking nearness and sincerity—which is the means for the acceptance of other deeds—is revived in him; otherwise, the rest of his deeds will be tainted and corrupted and will fall from the level of validity.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, 252. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, 136. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letters (Testament), 47.

8- Regardless of its content, prayer, in view of its conditions of validity, invites the purification of life. We know that the place of the prayer, the clothes of the prayer, the carpet on which he prays, the water with which he performs wudu and ghusl, and the place where the ghusl and wudu are performed must be free from any usurpation or violation of the rights of others. How can someone tainted by transgression, injustice, usury, usurpation, short-changing, bribery, and the acquisition of forbidden wealth prepare the preliminaries of prayer?

Therefore, the repetition of prayer five times a day and night is itself an invitation to respect the rights of others.

9- In addition to the conditions of validity, prayer has conditions of acceptance, or in other words, conditions of perfection, the observance of which is another effective factor for abandoning many sins.

In books of jurisprudence and sources of Hadith, many things are mentioned as obstacles to the acceptance of prayer, including the issue of drinking wine, as stated in narrations: "The prayer of the wine-drinker will not be accepted for forty days unless he repents." [^1] And in several narrations, we read: "Among those whose prayer will not be accepted is the tyrannical leader." [^2] In some other narrations, it is explicitly stated that the prayer of one who does not pay Zakat will not be accepted, as well as other narrations saying that eating forbidden food or vanity and self-centeredness are obstacles to the acceptance of prayer. It is clear to what extent providing these conditions for acceptance is constructive.

[^1]: Bihar, Vol. 84, pp. 317 and 320. [^2]: Bihar, Vol. 84, p. 318.

10- Prayer strengthens the spirit of discipline in man, because it must be performed exactly at specific times, where both delaying it and advancing it cause the prayer to be void. Likewise, other etiquettes and rules regarding intention, standing, sitting, bowing, and prostration, the observance of which makes the acceptance of discipline in life's programs completely easy.

All these are benefits that exist in prayer, regardless of the issue of congregation (jama'ah). If we add the feature of congregation to it—for the spirit of prayer is that very congregation—it has countless other blessings that this is not the place to explain; moreover, everyone is more or less aware of them.

We conclude our discourse on the philosophy and secrets of prayer with a comprehensive Hadith narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام:

The Imam, in response to a letter asking about the philosophy of prayer, replied: "The reason for the legislation of prayer is that it is an attention to and acknowledgment of the Lordship of the Master, a struggle against polytheism and idolatry, standing in the presence of the Lord in ultimate humility and ultimate modesty, confession of sins and requesting forgiveness for past transgressions, and placing the forehead on the ground every day for the glorification of the Lord.

And also the goal is that man remains always alert and mindful, that the dust of forgetfulness does not settle on his heart, that he does not become intoxicated and arrogant, that he remains humble and modest, and that he becomes a seeker and enthusiast for an increase in the bounties of religion and the world.

In addition, the continuity of the remembrance of God during the night and day, which is achieved in the light of prayer, causes man not to forget his Master, Manager, and Creator, and that the spirit of rebellion and tyranny does not overcome him.

And this very attention to God and standing before Him prohibits man from transgressions and prevents various types of corruption." [^1]

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 3, p. 4.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَا تُجَـٰدِلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَقُولُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَأُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُنَا وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَٰحِدٌ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (٤٦)
Do not argue with the People of the Book except in a manner which is best, except such of them as are wrongdoers, and say, ‘We believe in what has been sent down to us and in what has been sent down to you; our God and your God is one [and the same], and to Him do we submit.’ (46)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۖ وَمِنْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٧)
Thus We have sent down the Book to you; those to whom We have given the Book believe in it, and of these there are some who believe in it, and none contests Our signs except the faithless. (47)
وَمَا كُنتَ تَتْلُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ وَلَا تَخُطُّهُۥ بِيَمِينِكَ ۖ إِذًا لَّٱرْتَابَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٤٨)
You did not use to recite any scripture before it, nor did you write it with your right hand, for then the impugners would have been skeptical. (48)
بَلْ هُوَ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ فِى صُدُورِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٤٩)
Indeed, it is [present as] manifest signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge, and none contests Our signs except wrongdoers. (49)

Commentary

Choose the Best Method for Debate

In the preceding verses, the discourse primarily focused on how to confront the stubborn and ignorant "idolaters," speaking to them—as necessitated by the circumstances—with sharp logic and describing their deities as flimsier than a spider’s web. However, in the verses under discussion, the topic shifts to debating the "People of the Book," which must be conducted in a gentler manner. This is because they had, at the very least, heard a portion of the instructions of the Prophets and the Heavenly Books and possessed a greater readiness for logical discourse; for one must speak to every person according to the measure of their intellect, knowledge, and ethics.

First, He says: "And do not argue with the People of the Book except by that which is best" (وَلَا تُجَادِلُوا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ) [^1]. The word la tujadilu is derived from the root jidal, which originally means to twist a rope and tighten it. This term is also used regarding a sturdy building and the like. When two people engage in a discussion, and in reality, each wishes to "twist" the other away from their opinion, this act is called mujadalah (debate/argument). Wrestling is also sometimes called jidal. In any case, what is meant here is logical discussion and dialogue.

The expression "by that which is best" (bi-llati hiya ahsan) is a very comprehensive phrase that encompasses all correct and appropriate methods of debating, whether in the choice of words, the content of the speech, the tone of voice, or other accompanying gestures.

Therefore, the concept of this sentence is that your words should be polite, the tone of speech friendly, the content reasoned, and the pitch of the voice free from shouting, clamor, any form of harshness, or violation of respect. Furthermore, the movements of the hands, eyes, and eyebrows—which usually complement human expression—must all be conducted within this same manner and method.

How beautiful are the expressions of the Quran, where a world of meaning is hidden within a single short sentence!

All of this is because the goal of discussion and debate is not to seek superiority, dominance, or to embarrass the opposing party; rather, the goal is for the words to be effective and to penetrate the depths of the other person's soul. The best way to achieve this goal is this very Quranic method.

Indeed, it often happens that if a person reflects a truthful statement in such a way that the opposing party considers it their own thought rather than the speaker's, they show flexibility sooner, for humans are as fond of their own thoughts as they are of their own children.

It is for this very reason that the Holy Quran presents many issues in the form of "questions and inquiries," so that the answer springs from within the listener's own mind and they consider it their own.

However, of course, every law has an exception. Among them is this general principle in Islamic debate; in some cases, it might be interpreted as weakness or abjectness, or the opposing party might be so intoxicated and arrogant that this humane approach only increases their audacity and insolence. Therefore, following the verse, He states as an exception: "except for those among them who commit injustice" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْهُمْ).

Those who committed injustice against themselves and others and concealed many of the Divine verses so that people would not become acquainted with the attributes of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Those who committed injustice and trampled upon Divine commands wherever they went against their interests.

Those who committed injustice and introduced superstitions like the polytheists, calling "Messiah" or "Ezra" the son of God.

And finally, those who committed injustice and, instead of logical debate, took up the sword, resorted to force, and engaged in mischief and plotting.

At the end of the verse, He brings forth one of the clear examples of "debating in the best manner," which can serve as a living model for this discussion, saying:

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 16, p. 300

"Say: 'We believe in that which has been revealed to us and revealed to you; and our God and your God is one; and to Him we are submissive'" (وَقُولُوا آمَنَّا بِالَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَأُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ وَإِلَهُنَا وَإِلَهُكُمْ وَاحِدٌ وَنَحْنُ لَهُ مُسْلِمُونَ).

What a beautiful expression and what a wonderful melody! The melody of unity and faith in all that has been revealed by the One God, the elimination of all prejudices and "us vs. them" mentalities, and finally, the Oneness of the Deity and unconditional submission before "Allah."

This is an example of "debating in the best manner" that captivates anyone who hears it. It shows that Islam is not factionalist or divisive; the voice of Islam is the voice of unity and submission to every word of truth.

Examples of this type of discussion are abundant in the Quran. Among them is an example pointed out by Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a hadith, where he says: "Debating in the best manner is like the matter mentioned at the end of Surah Ya-Sin regarding the deniers of the Resurrection. When they brought a decayed bone before the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: 'Who has the power to revive this?' He said: 'He will give them life who produced them the first time...' (يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَهَا أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ) the same God who created it on the first day will revive it; the same God who produces fire for you from the green tree." [^2]

The next verse, as an emphasis on the four principles mentioned in the previous verse, says: "And thus We have sent down the Book (the Quran) to you" (وَكَذَلِكَ أَنْزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ). Yes, this Quran has been revealed based on the Oneness of the Deity, the unity of the call of all true Prophets, unconditional submission to the command of Truth, and debating with the best methods.

Some commentators have said: The meaning of the above sentence is to liken the revelation of the Quran to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to the revelation of previous Books to other Prophets; meaning, just as We revealed Heavenly Books to past Prophets, We have revealed the Quran to you as well.

However, the first interpretation seems more precise, although combining both meanings is also possible.

Then He adds: "Those to whom We gave the Scripture before this (and are truly committed and believe in it) believe in it" (فَالَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ). This is because they have both found its signs in their own Books and see its content, in terms of general principles, as being in harmony with the content of their own Books.

Of course, we know that not all the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) believed in the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). Therefore, the above sentence refers to that group of true believers and truth-seekers free from prejudice, for whom the title "People of the Book" is truly fitting.

Then He adds: "And of these (the people of Mecca and the Arab polytheists) there are some who believe in it" (وَمِنْ هَؤُلَاءِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِهِ) [^3]. And at the end, regarding the disbelievers of both groups, He says: "And none reject Our verses except the disbelievers" (وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَّا الْكَافِرُونَ).

Considering that the concept of jahd (rejection) is for a person to believe in something yet deny it [^4], the meaning of the above sentence becomes that even the disbelievers acknowledge the greatness of these verses in their hearts and see the signs of sincerity and truth upon them. They consider the way of life of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), his pure life, and his devoted followers as proof of its authenticity; however, due to stubbornness, prejudice, blind imitation of ancestors, or to preserve fleeting illegitimate interests, they rise up in denial.

In this way, the Quran identifies the positions of various groups toward this Heavenly Book: on one side are the people of faith, including the scholars of the People of the Book, true believers, and polytheists who were thirsty for the truth, found it, and set their hearts upon it. On the other side are the stubborn deniers who saw the truth but, like bats, hid themselves from it, because the darkness of disbelief has become part of the fabric of their being and they dread the light of faith!

[^1]: Al-lati is an adjective for an omitted noun like al-tariqah (the method). [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 163. [^3]: Some commentators have considered the phrase "Those to whom We gave the Scripture" to refer to the Muslims, and the phrase "And of these there are some who believe in it" to refer to the People of the Book, but this interpretation seems very unlikely, as the expression "Those to whom We gave the Scripture" and its likes are apparently never applied in the Quran to anyone other than the Jews and Christians. [^4]: Raghib in al-Mufradat says: Al-juhud is the negation of what is established in the heart and the affirmation of what is negated in the heart.

It is noteworthy that this group was already disbelieving, but the renewed emphasis on their disbelief might be because the proof (itmam al-hujjah) had not been fully established for them before. True disbelief is now, when the proof has been established for them, yet with knowledge and awareness, they have abandoned the Straight Path and are walking in error.

Then, He points to another clear sign of the truthfulness of the call of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), which is an emphasis on the content of the previous verse, saying: "And you did not recite before it any scripture, nor did you inscribe it with your right hand, otherwise the falsifiers would have doubted" (meaning they would say that what he brought is the result of studying previous books and copying from them) (وَمَا كُنْتَ تَتْلُوا مِنْ قَبْلِهِ مِنْ كِتَابٍ وَلَا تَخُطُّهُ بِيَمِينِكَ إِذًا لَارْتَابَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ) [^5]. You never went to school and never wrote, yet by the sign of Divine revelation, you became the teacher of a hundred schools!
