How can it be believed that an unlettered person who never saw a teacher or a school could, by his own power, bring a book and challenge the entire human world to match it, and everyone would be unable to produce the like of it?

Is this not proof that your power is aided by the infinite power of the Lord? And that your Book is a Heavenly revelation bestowed upon you from His side?

It is necessary to pay attention to this point: if someone asks, "How do we know that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) never went to school and never wrote?" we answer: He lived in an environment where literacy was extremely limited and scarce, such that it is said in the entire city of Mecca, no more than 17 people had the ability to read and write. In such an environment, if someone were to study or attend a school, it would be impossible to conceal it; it would become famous and well-known everywhere, and his teacher and lessons would be identified.

How could such a person claim to be a true Prophet while telling such an obvious lie? Especially since these verses were revealed in Mecca, the cradle of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) upbringing, and in the face of stubborn enemies who would not miss the smallest weakness.

In the next verse, He states other signs for the truthfulness of the Quran, saying: "Rather, it is clear verses [preserved] within the breasts of those who have been given knowledge" (بَلْ هُوَ آيَاتٌ بَيِّنَاتٌ فِي صُدُورِ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ).

The expression "clear verses" (ayatun bayyinat) reflects the reality that the signs of the Quran's truthfulness are visible within itself and shine upon the face of the verses; its proof is within itself.

In truth, it is like the cosmic signs (ayat takwini) from which a person realizes the truth by studying them without needing anything else. These legislative verses (ayat tashri'i) are also such that, in terms of appearance and content, they are their own proof of sincerity.

Beyond this, the supporters of these verses and those who seek and love them are those who possess a share of knowledge and awareness, even if their hands are empty and their feet are bare.

To put it more clearly, one of the ways to recognize the authenticity of a school of thought is to examine the state of the believers in that school. If a group of ignorant people or charlatans surround someone, it would seem that he too is of the same ilk. But if those in whose breasts the secrets of sciences are hidden declare loyalty to a school, it is proof of its truthfulness. And we see that a group of scholars of the People of the Book and distinguished pious personalities like Abu Dharr, Salman, Miqdad, Ammar ibn Yasir, and a supreme personality like Ali (عليه السلام) were the supporters and lovers of this school.

In many narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام), this verse has been interpreted as referring to the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام). This does not imply exclusivity, but rather identifies a clear instance of "those who have been given knowledge" [^6]. If we see in some narrations it is explicitly stated that it refers specifically to the Imams, it is in fact an allusion to the complete stage of the knowledge of the Quran which is in their possession. There is no obstacle to scholars and scientists, or even the enlightened masses of people, having a share of this Quranic knowledge.

Furthermore, this verse shows that knowledge is not limited to what is read in books or learned in the presence of a teacher, for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), according to the explicit text of the previous verses, did not go to school and did not write, yet he was the supreme instance of "those who have been given knowledge." Thus, beyond formal knowledge, there is a higher and more sublime knowledge that is cast by the Lord as a light into the human heart: "Knowledge is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills." This is the essence of knowledge, and other sciences are but the shell.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "And none reject Our verses except the wrongdoers" (وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَّا الظَّالِمُونَ).

For its signs are clear: an unlettered and unschooled Prophet brought it.

The enlightened thinkers are the believers in it.

[^5]: The pronoun in min qablihi refers back to the Quran, and the expression "right hand" (yamin) is because humans mostly write with their right hand. Mubtilun is the plural of mubtil, meaning one who seeks to falsify or invalidate something. [^6]: These narrations are detailed in Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 254 onwards.

Beyond this, it is itself a collection of clear verses (words with clear and manifest content).

And its signs have also appeared in previous Books.

With all this, does anyone deny it except those who wrong themselves and society? (We repeat that the term jahd is used when a person knows something but denies it contrary to their knowledge).

Key Points

1- My Beloved who never went to school! ...

It is true that reading and writing are considered a perfection for every human being, but sometimes circumstances arise where not reading and not writing is a perfection. This applies perfectly to the Prophets, especially the Seal of the Prophets (صلى الله عليه وآله). This is because if a schooled scholar and an aware, well-read philosopher were to claim prophethood and present a book as a Heavenly Book, in such circumstances, doubts and hesitations might arise as to whether this book and school are the product of his own thoughts.

But if we see that from among an underdeveloped people, a human being who never sat before a teacher, never read a book, and never wrote a page, arose and presented a book as grand as the universe, with very high and sublime content, here it can be very well understood that this is not the outpouring of his brain, but rather Heavenly revelation and Divine teaching.

In other verses of the Quran, the "unlettered" (ummi) nature of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is also emphasized. As we mentioned under verse 157 of Surah al-A'raf, three interpretations have been mentioned for the word ummi, the clearest of which is "unschooled."

Fundamentally, in the environment of the Hejaz, there were no lessons for the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to study and no teacher whose presence he could benefit from. We mentioned that the number of people who had even the basic literacy of reading and writing in all of Mecca among men did not exceed 17. It is said that among women, there was only one who knew how to read and write [^7]. It is natural that in such an environment where the most basic stage of knowledge (reading and writing) is so rare and limited, it is impossible for someone to have studied and for the people not to be aware of it. If someone decisively claimed, "I have had no schooling," and no one denied it, it is clear proof of the truth of his statement. In any case, this specific situation of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) mentioned in the above verses was very effective and useful for completing the miracle of the Quran and cutting off the excuses of the excuse-seekers. Yes, he was a great and peerless scholar who had only studied in the school of revelation.

The only excuse remaining for some is that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), before his prophethood, made one or two trips to Syria (for a short period while engaged in trade). They say perhaps during these one or two trips he contacted the scholars of the People of the Book and received these matters from them! The weakness of this claim is inherent within it: how is it possible for an unschooled person who never went to school to hear so much history of the Prophets, laws, regulations, and high gnosis from individuals in such a short time, memorize them, and implement them over 23 years? And to show the necessary reaction when faced with unprecedented and unexpected events? This is exactly like saying that a certain great scientist learned all the sciences and techniques of medicine in those few days he was observing the treatment of patients by doctors in a certain hospital; such a statement is more like a joke!

It is also necessary to pay attention to this point: after reaching the stage of

[^7]: Futuh al-Buldan, Baladhuri, Egypt edition, p. 459.

prophethood, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) likely had the ability to write and read through Divine instruction, although it has never been seen in any history that he made use of this knowledge—to read something from a text or to write a letter with his own hand. Perhaps the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) avoidance of this throughout his life was again so as not to give a pretext to the excuse-seekers.

The only case mentioned in some history and hadith books where the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself wrote something is in the event of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, which appears in Musnad Ahmad: "The Prophet personally took the pen and wrote the peace treaty" [^8]. However, a group of Islamic scholars have denied this hadith and consider it in explicit contradiction to the above verses, although in the view of some, it is not explicit, because these verses refer to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) state before prophethood. What obstacle is there for him to exceptionally write a script in one instance after attaining the station of prophethood, which would itself be considered a miracle?

In any case, in such a matter, relying on a single report (khabar wahid) is far from caution and contrary to what has been proven in the science of Usul, even if this report, as we said, does not create a problem [^9].

2- The Way to Influence Others

To conquer hearts and for the word of truth to influence the thoughts of others, merely resorting to strong and powerful arguments is not enough. The manner of dealing with the other party and the method of debate leave the deepest effect

[^8]: Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 4, p. 298. [^9]: In Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 400 onwards, there is another explanation regarding the "unlettered Prophet" (under verse 157 of Surah al-A'raf).

at this stage. There are many who are precise and meticulous in debates and are dominant and aware of scientific issues, but because they are unaware of the method of "best debate" (jidal ahsan) and constructive discussions, they are less successful in influencing the hearts of others when talking to them.

The truth is that satisfying the "intellect and thought" alone is not enough; the "emotions" must also be satisfied, as they constitute half of human existence.

In other words, influencing the "conscious" stage of the human soul alone is not sufficient; one must also influence the "unconscious" stage, which constitutes a major part of the soul.

From examining the lives of the Prophets, especially the life of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams of Guidance (علیهم السلام), it is well understood that these noble figures used social ethics, psychological principles, and the most humane methods of influencing hearts to realize their missionary and educational goals.

Their way of dealing with people was such that they quickly attracted and absorbed them toward their goals. Although some wish to always give these matters a miraculous aspect, it is not so. If we also apply their tradition, method, and style of debate in dealing with others, we can quickly affect them and penetrate the depths of their souls.

The Quran explicitly says to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "So by mercy from Allah, you were lenient with them. And if you had been rude [in speech] and harsh in heart, they would have disbanded from about you" (Al-Imran: 159).

It has often been seen that some, after hours of discussion and dialogue, not only achieve no progress in their negotiations but see the other party more rooted

and prejudiced in their false belief. The reason for this is that the method of "best debate" was not used.

Harshness in debate, seeking superiority, belittling the opposing party, showing pride and arrogance, lack of respect for the thoughts of others, and lack of sincerity in discussions are all matters that cause a person to fail in debate. Therefore, in the discussions of Islamic ethics, we see a topic titled the prohibition of jidal (contention) and mira' (disputation), which refers to discussions in which the spirit of truth-seeking is absent, but rather the goal is aggression, seeking superiority, and imposing one's own word! The prohibition of jidal and mira', beyond spiritual and ethical aspects, is for this very reason: that in such discussions, intellectual progress is never achieved.

The prohibition of jidal and mira' are close to each other, but Islamic scholars have placed differences between them: mira' means showing off one's excellence and perfection, and jidal is for the purpose of belittling the opposing party.

Jidal is said of initial attacks in a debate, while mira' is said of defensive attacks.

Jidal is in scientific matters, and mira' is more general (of course, there is no contradiction between these interpretations).

In any case, arguing and debating with others is sometimes "best debate" (jidal bi-l-ahsan), which is a discussion in which the conditions we mentioned above are strictly observed, and sometimes it is "other than best," which is when the above matters are forgotten.

We end this discourse by mentioning a few expressive and instructive narrations.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "No servant completes the reality of faith until he leaves mira', even if he is in the right." [^10]

In another hadith, we read that the Prophet of God, Solomon, said to his son: "O my son! Beware of mira', for there is no benefit in it, and it stirs up enmity between brothers." [^11]

And it is also narrated from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said: "No people went astray after they were guided except that they were given to jidal (contentious debate where truth is not sought)." [^12]

3- "Disbelievers" and "Wrongdoers"

In the above verses, we encounter the phrase "And none reject Our verses except the disbelievers" once, and another time the same phrase with the difference that the title "wrongdoers" (zalimun) takes the place of "disbelievers," saying: "And none reject Our verses except the wrongdoers."

Comparing these two shows that the matter is not a case of repetition, but rather the expression of two different points: one refers to ideological aspects and the other to practical aspects.

First, it says: "Those who, with their incorrect preconceptions or blind imitation of ancestors, have chosen disbelief and polytheism, will negate any sign they see from God, even if their intellect has correctly accepted it."

In the second expression, it says: Those who, by wrongdoing and injustice against themselves and society, have chosen a path and see their illegitimate interests in it, and are determined to continue this oppression—it is natural that they do not submit to Our verses, for Our verses, just as they are not compatible with their line of thought, are also not compatible with their line of action.

[^10]: Safinat al-Bihar, root m-r-a. [^11]: and [^12]: Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din.

They ask you to hasten the punishment, and indeed hell will besiege the faithless (54)
يَوْمَ يَغْشَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٥٥)
on the day when the punishment envelopes them, from above them and from under their feet, and He will say, ‘Taste what you used to do!’ (55)

Commentary

Is the Miracle of the Qur'an Not Enough?!

Those who, due to stubbornness and persistence in falsehood, were unwilling to submit at any price to the reasoned and logical discourse of the Qur'an, and refused to accept that the bringing of a book like the Qur'an by an unlettered individual like the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was a clear proof of his truthfulness, resorted to a new pretext. As the Qur'an states in the first verses under discussion: out of mockery and derision, "they said, 'Why have miracles (like the miracles of Moses and Jesus) not been sent down to him from his Lord?'"

(وَ قالُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آياتٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

Why does he not possess the staff of Moses, the "White Hand," and the life-giving breath of the Messiah?

Why does he not destroy his enemies with great miracles, just as Moses, Shu'ayb, Hud, Noah, and Thamud did?

Or, as narrated from this group in Surah Al-Isra: "Why does the Prophet of Islam not cause rivers and springs of flowing water to appear from the dry desert of Mecca?" "Why does he not have a palace of gold?" "Why does he not ascend to heaven?" and "Why does he not bring a letter from God from the sky for them!!" [1] Undoubtedly, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) possessed other miracles besides the Holy Qur'an, and history speaks of them explicitly. However, with these words, they were not seeking to find a miracle; rather, on one hand, they wanted to ignore the miraculous nature of the Qur'an, and on the other, they were demanding "provisional miracles" (by which is meant that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) should perform any supernatural act they proposed according to their whims—for instance, one would suggest bringing forth springs of water, another would say 'I won't accept unless you turn the mountains of Mecca into gold,' and a third would complain that this is not enough and he must ascend to heaven; in this way, they would turn miracles into a worthless plaything, and even after seeing all of them, they would ultimately call him a sorcerer).

Therefore, the Qur'an says in verse 111 of Surah Al-An'am: (وَ لَوْ أَنَّنا نَزَّلْنا إِلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةَ وَ كَلَّمَهُمُ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ حَشَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قُبُلًا ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا): "Even if We had sent down to them the angels and the dead had spoken to them and We had gathered everything before them [face to face], they would not have believed!" In any case, to respond to these stubborn pretext-seekers, the Qur'an approaches from two paths:

[1]: Surah Al-Isra, verses 90 to 93.

First, it says: "Say to them, 'Miracles are not my work to be performed according to your desires; miracles are all with Allah'" (قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْآياتُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌).

He knows what miracle is appropriate for what time and for which people. He knows which individuals are seeking to research and pursue the truth—to whom supernatural signs must be shown—and which individuals are mere pretext-seekers following their carnal desires.

And say, "I am only a clear warner" (وَ إِنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

My only duty is to warn, to convey the message, and to articulate the Word of God; however, the presentation of miracles and supernatural acts is solely at the discretion of His Pure Essence. This is one response.

The other response is: "Is it not enough for them that We have sent down to you the Book which is recited to them?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ أَنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ‌). They demand physical miracles, while the Qur'an is the supreme spiritual miracle.

They demand a fleeting miracle, while the Qur'an is an eternal miracle, and its verses are recited to them day and night.

Is it possible for an unlettered man—and even if we assume he were educated—to bring a book with such content and such extraordinary attraction that is beyond human capability, and challenge the entire world to match it, yet everyone remains helpless and incapable before it?! If their intention is truly a miracle, We have provided them with more than they requested through the revelation of the Qur'an; but no, they are not seekers of truth, they are seekers of pretexts.

It should be noted that the phrase (أَ وَ لَمْ يَكْفِهِمْ) ("Is it not enough for them?") is usually used in cases where someone has performed a task beyond the other party's expectation, yet the other party is either oblivious to it or pretends to be. For example, one might ask, "Why did you not do such-and-such service for me?" and we point to a much greater service that he has ignored, saying, "Is it not enough that we did such a great service for you?!" Beyond all this, a miracle must be in harmony with the conditions of time and place and the nature of the Prophet's call. A Prophet whose religion is eternal must have an eternal miracle.

A Prophet whose call is global and must encompass future centuries and ages must have a clear spiritual and intellectual miracle that attracts the thoughts of all thinkers and sages. Certainly, for such a purpose, the Qur'an is suitable, not the staff of Moses or the White Hand.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis and clarification, it says: "Indeed, in this [Book] is a great mercy and a clear reminder for a people who believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَرَحْمَةً وَ ذِكْرى‌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Yes, the Qur'an is both a mercy and a means of remembrance, but for the group of believers—for those who have opened the doors of their hearts to the truth, for those who seek light and wish to find the way. They feel this divine mercy with their entire being and find tranquility in its light; every time they read the verses of the Qur'an, they find a fresh reminder.

The difference between "mercy" (رحمة) and "reminder" (ذكرى) might be that the Qur'an is not merely a miracle and a source of remembrance; rather, in addition to that, it is full of mercy-generating programs and laws, and educational and human-building instructions. For instance, the staff of Moses was only a miracle and had no effect on the daily lives of the people, but the Qur'an is both a miracle and a complete program for life and a source of mercy.

And since every claimant needs a witness and evidence, in the next verse He says: "Say, 'Allah is sufficient as a witness between me and you'" (قُلْ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ شَهِيداً). It is obvious that the more knowledgeable a witness is, the greater the value of his testimony. Therefore, in the following phrase, He adds: "[The witness is] the God who knows all that is in the heavens and the earth" (يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Now let us see how Allah has testified to the truthfulness of His Prophet.

This testimony might be a practical testimony, for when Allah places a great miracle like the Qur'an at the disposal of His Prophet, He has signed the document of his truthfulness. Is it possible for the Wise and Just God to place a miracle at the disposal of a liar (God forbid)? Therefore, granting the miracle to the person of the Prophet is itself the best way for God to testify to his prophethood.

In addition to the practical testimony above, verbal testimony has also been given in numerous verses of the Holy Qur'an, as we read in verse 40 of Surah Al-Ahzab: (ما كانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبا أَحَدٍ مِنْ رِجالِكُمْ وَ لكِنْ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ وَ خاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ‌).

And in verse 29 of Surah Al-Fath, it is also stated: (مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ‌): "Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; and those with him are forceful against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves."

Some commentators have said: This verse was revealed in response to some leaders of the Jews in Medina, such as Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf and his followers, who said: "O Muhammad, who testifies that you are the messenger of God?" The verse was revealed and said: Allah gives such testimony.

From here, another interpretation and explanation for the above verse can also be obtained, which is that it refers to the testimony and witnessing of Allah in the previous heavenly books, of which the scholars of the People of the Book were well aware.

At the same time, there is no contradiction between these three interpretations, and it is possible that all are included in the meaning of the verse.

At the end of the verse, as a warning and threat, He says: "And those who believe in falsehood and disbelieve in Allah—it is they who are the real losers" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْباطِلِ وَ كَفَرُوا بِاللَّهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ‌).

What loss is greater than for a person to lose all the capital of his existence for nothing? Just as the polytheists did; they placed their hearts and souls at the disposal of idols and spent all their physical strength, and social and individual resources, on promoting and propagating the creed of idolatry and erasing the name of Allah, and they gained nothing but loss and detriment.

The Qur'an frequently refers to this great loss in its verses, and sometimes by using the word "Akhsar" (the greatest losers), it shows this reality that there is no loss greater or higher than this (Hud: 22, An-Naml: 5, Al-Kahf: 103).

More importantly, sometimes a person loses in a transaction and only loses his capital and goes bankrupt, but sometimes it goes beyond this and a large debt also remains on his shoulders; this is the worst type of bankruptcy. The polytheists are exactly in this state; rather, they sometimes become the cause of the bankruptcy and misguidance of others as well, forming what could be called a "chain bankruptcy." [1]

[1]: In this regard, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 12, page 563 (under verse 103 of Surah Al-Kahf).

In the previous verses, two parts of the disbelievers' pretexts against the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call and the responses to them were discussed. First, they said: "Why does he not bring a miracle?" To which the Qur'an replied: "This heavenly Book is itself the supreme miracle."

Second: "Who testifies to his truthfulness?" To which the Qur'an replied: "The God who is aware of all things."

In the verse under discussion, it refers to their third pretext. It says, "They challenge you to hasten the punishment" (وَ يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ‌).

They say, "If the divine punishment is true and will overtake the disbelievers, then why does it not come to us?!" The Qur'an gives three answers to this statement.

First, it says: "Had it not been for a term decreed, the punishment would have come to them immediately" (وَ لَوْ لا أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى‌ لجاءهم العذاب).

This appointed time is so that the primary goal—namely, awakening or the completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah) against them—is achieved. God never hastens or rushes in His actions contrary to wisdom.

Second, what certainty do those who say this have that the divine punishment will not overtake them at any moment? "For this punishment will come to them suddenly and without warning, while they perceive not and are unaware" (وَ لَيَأْتِيَنَّهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌). [1] Although the time of punishment is indeed fixed and decreed, the wisdom lies in them not being aware of it so that it arrives without warning. For if its time were announced, it would lead to the audacity and boldness of the disbelievers and sinners; they would continue in sin and disbelief until the last moment, and only when the decreed time of punishment approached would they all repent and return to the truth! The educational philosophy of these punishments requires that their timing be hidden so that they exert their effect at every moment, and the fear and dread of it become a deterrent. Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of the phrase (وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ‌) is not that they do not perceive the existence of the punishment itself—otherwise, the philosophy of punishment would be lost—but rather it means they do not recognize the moment of its occurrence and its preliminaries; in other words, it descends upon them unexpectedly like a lightning bolt.

It appears from various verses of the Qur'an that this pretext-seeking was not limited to the disbelievers of Mecca; rather, many other nations also insisted on the issue of hastening the punishment.

Finally, the Qur'an states the third response in the next verse, saying: "They challenge you to hasten the punishment, while indeed Hell encompasses the disbelievers!" (يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ‌). If the punishment of this world is delayed, the punishment of the hereafter is one hundred percent certain. It is so definite that the Qur'an speaks of it as a present reality and says, "Hell, as if it already encompasses them!" Here, there is also a more precise interpretation for this verse, which is that Hell encompasses this group right now "in the true sense of the word" from two perspectives.

First is the hell of this world: due to shirk (polytheism) and being tainted by sin, they burn in a hell they have prepared for themselves—the hell of war and bloodshed, the hell of conflict and discord, the hell of insecurity and unrest, the hell of oppression and injustice, and the hell of rebellious whims and desires! Second, according to the apparent meaning of the Qur'anic verses, Hell already exists, and according to the analysis we have previously provided, it is within the inner reality of this world, and thus it truly encompasses the disbelievers. This is also referred to in verses 5, 6, and 7 of Surah At-Takathur: (كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ‌): "No! If you only knew with the knowledge of certainty... You will surely see the Hellfire. Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty." [1]

Then it adds: "The day when the divine punishment covers them from above them and from beneath their feet, and He says, 'Taste what you used to do'—it is a hard and painful day" (يَوْمَ يَغْشاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ وَ يَقُولُ ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌). [2] This verse might be an explanation for the encompassing of the punishment of Hell in the Hereafter for the disbelievers, or it might be considered an independent description of that painful punishment of theirs which encompasses them today due to their actions and will become manifest and apparent tomorrow. In any case, the fact that He says "this punishment is from above the head and beneath the feet" and does not mention the other directions and sides is actually for the sake of the clarity of the matter and the lucidity of the discussion. Moreover, when the flames of fire flare up from beneath the feet and pour down from above upon their heads, they will encompass their entire bodies and cover all sides and surroundings.

[1]: For further explanation in this regard, please refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 92 onwards (under verse 133 of Surah Al-Imran). [2]: Some commentators have considered "Day" (يوم) as an adverb for an omitted verb, while others consider it related to "encompassing" (محيطة).

Fundamentally, this expression is common in both Persian and Arabic, where it is said that someone, for example, sank from head to toe in the quagmire of immorality, meaning their entire being was drowned in this sin. In this way, the problem that has arisen for some commentators—as to why only above and below are mentioned and the four sides are left silent—is resolved.

The phrase (ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌) ("Taste what you used to do"), whose speaker is apparently Allah, is not only a type of psychological punishment for such persons but also expresses the reality that divine punishment is nothing but the reflection and manifestation of man's own actions in the realm of the Hereafter.

Key Points

1- Proofs of the Miraculous Nature of the Qur'an Undoubtedly, the Qur'an is the greatest miracle of the Prophet of Islam; it is a speaking, eternal, and dynamic miracle, suitable for every age and time and for all classes of people. We have provided detailed discussions regarding the miraculous nature of the Qur'an in Volume 1 under verse 23 of Surah Al-Baqarah and see no need for repetition.

2- A Pretext for Denying Miracles Some Westernized Islamic scholars who wish to ignore the supernatural acts of the Prophets insist that the Prophet of Islam had no miracle other than the Qur'an; they might even not consider the Qur'an a miracle. However, this statement is contrary to the verses of the Qur'an, the mutawatir (frequently narrated) traditions, and established Islamic history. We explained the details of this in Volume 12, page 386 (under verses 90-93 of Surah Al-Isra).

3- Provisional Miracles One of the stubborn methods of the opponents of the Prophets has always been the proposal of "provisional miracles." By doing this, they wanted, on one hand, to break the awe of miracles and reduce them to triviality, and on the other, to have a pretext for not accepting the call of the Prophets. However, the Divine Prophets never submitted to these conspiracies, and as we saw in the verses above, they replied: "Miracles are not in our hands to be performed according to your whims every day and every hour; rather, miracles only take place by the command of God and are outside our control." A description regarding provisional miracles is provided in Volume 8, page 253 onwards (under verse 20 of Surah Yunus).

Ayahs

يَـٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ أَرْضِى وَٰسِعَةٌ فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٥٦)
O My servants who have faith! My earth is indeed vast. So worship [only] Me. (56)
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ (٥٧)
Every soul shall taste death. Then you shall be brought back to Us. (57)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ غُرَفًا تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ نِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (٥٨)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We will surely settle them in the lofty abodes of paradise, with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. How excellent is the reward of the workers! (58)
ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٥٩)
—Those who are patient and who put their trust in their Lord. (59)
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن دَآبَّةٍ لَّا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا ٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُهَا وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦٠)
How many an animal there is that does not carry its own provision. Allah provides them and you, and He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (60)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators believe that the first verse was revealed regarding the believers who were under severe pressure from the disbelievers in Mecca, to the extent that they were unable to perform their Islamic duties. They were commanded to emigrate from that land.

Furthermore, some commentators believe that the verse: وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ دَابَّةٍ لا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا (the last verse under discussion) was revealed concerning a group of believers who were suffering from the persecution of enemies in Mecca. They would say: "If we emigrate to Medina, we will have neither a house nor land there; who will then provide us with food and water?!" (The verse was revealed stating that all living creatures on earth receive their sustenance from the table of the Great Lord's bounty; do not grieve over your provision).

Commentary

An Emigration Must Be Made!

Since the previous verses spoke of the various stances of the polytheists against Islam and Muslims, the verses under discussion address the situation of the Muslims themselves. They describe their responsibility and duty regarding one of the problems they face in connection with the disbelievers—namely, the problem of harassment, restriction, and pressure.

It says: "O My servants who have believed, and are currently under the pressure of the enemy in performing your religious duties, My earth is vast; emigrate to another place and worship Me" (يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ أَرْضِي واسِعَةٌ فَإِيَّايَ فَاعْبُدُونِ).

It is evident that this is not an exclusive law pertaining only to the believers of Mecca. The occasion of revelation never limits the broad and expansive meaning of the verse, which is consistent with other verses of the Quran. Thus, in every age and era, and in every environment and place, if freedom is completely stripped from Muslims and staying there results in nothing but humiliation, abasement, and distance from Divine programs, it is the duty of Muslims to migrate to regions where they can attain absolute or relative freedom.

In other words: the purpose of human creation is the servitude of Allah—that very servitude which is the secret of freedom, honor, and human victory on all fronts. This is alluded to in the phrase "فَإِيَّايَ فَاعْبُدُونِ" (so worship Me alone), and it also appears in verse 56 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat: وَ ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ ("And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me").

Whenever this fundamental and ultimate goal is trampled upon, there is no way but emigration. Allah's earth is vast, and one must step toward another point. In such cases, one must never become a prisoner to concepts such as tribe, nation, homeland, or house and hearth, nor submit to humiliation and captivity; for the respect of these matters lasts only as long as the primary goal is not endangered.

It is in such instances that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: Laysa baladun bi-ahaqqa bika min baladin, khayru al-biladi ma hamalaka: "No city is more rightful to you than another; the best of cities is the one that welcomes you and provides the means for your progress." [1] It is true that love for one's homeland and affection for one's birthplace are part of every human's nature, but sometimes more important issues arise in life that overshadow this subject.

Regarding the Islamic perspective on the issue of migration and the narrations received in this field, we provided a detailed explanation in Volume 4, page 190 onwards (under verse 100 of Surah An-Nisa).

The expression "O My servants!" (يا عبادى) is the most affectionate expression used by Allah toward His servants. It is a crown of honor even superior to the station of Messengership and Vicegerency, just as we always prioritize it over Messengership in the Tashahhud of prayer, saying: "I bear witness that Muhammad is His servant and His Messenger."

Interestingly, when Allah created Adam, He honored him with the title of "Vicegerent of Allah" (Khalifatullah), yet Satan did not despair of tempting him; he approached Adam, and what happened, happened. But when He praised him with the station of servitude (ubudiyyah), Satan knelt before Him and said: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ: "By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your chosen servants among them" (Surah Sad, verses 82 and 83).

Even Allah has guaranteed this matter: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ "Indeed, over My servants you have no authority" (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 42).

Therefore, the station of pure servitude is even higher and more sublime than the station of Divine Vicegerency on earth.

From what we have said, it becomes clearly evident that "servants" in the verse under discussion does not refer to all humans, but rather to the believing humans, and the phrase "those who have believed" is mentioned as an emphasis and clarification. [1]

Since one of the excuses of those who remained in the lands of polytheism and were unwilling to emigrate was that they feared leaving their homeland might expose them to the danger of death by enemies, hunger, or other factors—and additionally, they would suffer separation from relatives, kin, children, and their city—the Quran, in the following verse, gives them a comprehensive answer: "Ultimately, all humans die, and everyone tastes death, then to Us you shall be returned" (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ ثُمَّ إِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ). This world is not an abode of permanence for anyone; some must go sooner and some later. Separation from friends, children, and relatives will occur in any case. Why should a person stay in the land of polytheism and disbelief for the sake of these fleeting matters and carry the burden of humiliation and captivity just to live a few days longer?

Beyond all this, fear that your death may arrive while you are still in this land of polytheism and disbelief before you move to the land of faith and Islam. How painful is such a death?! Furthermore, do not think that death is the end of everything; death is the beginning of the true life of humans, for "all of you shall be returned to Us"—to the Great Lord and toward His endless bounties.

The next verse describes a portion of these bounties as follows: "Those who believed and performed righteous deeds, We shall surely settle them in lofty chambers of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ غُرَفاً تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ). [1]

They will reside in palaces surrounded on all sides by heavenly trees, with various rivers—which, according to other verses of the Quran, each have their own specific taste and appearance—flowing through those trees and beneath these palaces.

(Note that "ghuraf" is the plural of "ghurfah," meaning a high building that overlooks its surroundings).

Another distinction of the heavenly chambers is that they are not like the houses and palaces of this world where, before one has even rested a moment, the call to "depart" is sounded; rather, "they shall abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها).

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "How excellent is the reward of those who work [for Allah]" (نِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ).

A simple comparison between what was said about the disbelievers and sinners in the previous verses and what is mentioned in this verse clarifies the greatness of the believers' reward.

The disbelievers were amidst fire and punishment that enveloped them from head to toe, and as a reproach, it was said to them: "Taste what you used to do!"

But the believers are immersed in heavenly bounties, and the mercy of the Lord surrounds them from every side. Instead of reproachful sentences, they hear words that are all signs of the affection and grace of the Generous Lord. Yes, it is said to them: "How good and beautiful is the reward and prize of the workers."

It is obvious that "workers" ('amilin), by the context of the preceding sentences, refers to those who perform righteous deeds coupled with faith, even though the word "workers" is absolute.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, in Paradise there are chambers whose outside can be seen from their inside, and whose inside can be seen from their outside!" Someone stood up and asked: "For whom are these chambers, O Messenger of Allah?" He replied: Hiya liman ataba al-kalam wa at'ama al-ta'am wa adama al-siyam wa salla lillahi bi-l-layli wa al-nasu niyam: "These are for the one who purifies his speech, feeds the hungry, fasts frequently, and prays to Allah in the heart of the night while the people are asleep." [1]

The next verse describes the most important qualities of the "working believers" in this way: "They are those who exercise patience and steadfastness in the face of difficulties, and put their trust in their Lord" (الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ).

They separate from their wives, children, friends, relatives, and their homes and hearths, and they remain patient. They taste the bitterness of exile and the hardships of displacement from their homeland, and they are steadfast. To preserve their faith, they endure the harassment of enemies, and in the path of the struggle against the self—which is the "Greater Jihad"—and the struggle against fierce enemies—which is the "Lesser Jihad"—they welcome all kinds of difficulties and remain patient.

Yes, this patience and steadfastness is the secret of their victory and the great factor of their pride, without which no positive action in life is possible.

Furthermore, they rely neither on their wealth nor on their friends and relatives; their sole reliance is on Allah, and their trust is in His Pure Essence. If a thousand enemies intend to destroy them, they say: "If You are my friend, I have no fear of enemies!" And if we reflect correctly, the root of all human virtues is this "patience" and "trust" (tawakkul): patience is the factor of steadfastness against obstacles and difficulties, and trust is the motivation for movement on this path full of ups and downs.

In truth, to perform righteous deeds, one must seek help from these two moral virtues: patience and trust, for without these two, performing righteous deeds on a large scale is never possible. [1]

In the last verse under discussion, in response to those who said—either through their state of being or through their words—"If we emigrate from our city and homeland, who will provide us with sustenance?" The Quran says: Do not grieve over sustenance and do not accept the shame of humiliation and captivity; Allah is the Provider. Not only you, but "how many a creature there is that does not carry its own provision, never having a food store in its nest, and seeking new provision every new day, yet Allah does not leave them hungry and provides for them, and He provides for you as well" (وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ دَابَّةٍ لا تَحْمِلُ رِزْقَهَا اللَّهُ يَرْزُقُها وَ إِيَّاكُمْ).

Among the creatures, animals, and insects, apart from humans, there are few types—such as ants and honeybees—that carry and store their food from the fields and wilderness to their nests. Most are "sparrow-sustained," meaning they must seek fresh provision every new day. Millions upon millions of them exist around us in far and near places, in deserts and the depths of valleys, atop mountains, and within the seas, all eating from the table of His unsparing bounty.

And you, O human, who are more intelligent and capable than them in obtaining and storing provision, why do you cling so much to a tainted and shameful life out of fear of your sustenance being cut off? And why do you submit to every oppression, tyranny, abasement, and humiliation? You too, come out from within this narrow and dark boundary of your life, sit at the expansive table of your Lord, and do not grieve over sustenance!

On that day when you were imprisoned in your mother's womb as a weak and helpless embryo, and no one—not even your kind parents—had access to you, your Lord did not forget you and placed exactly what you needed at your disposal. Today, you are a capable and powerful being. And since providing sustenance to the needy is secondary to being aware of their existence and needs, He emphasizes at the end of the verse: "He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (وَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

He hears all your words, and He even knows the "language of the state" of you and all creatures. He is well aware of the needs of everyone, and nothing is hidden from the circle of His infinite knowledge.

[1] Short Sayings, No. 442. [1] The phrase "فَإِيَّايَ فَاعْبُدُونِ" is actually a conjunction to the result of a conditional sentence that is omitted, and its estimation is: "If the earth becomes narrow for you, then emigrate from it to another, and worship Me alone." [1] "Lanubawwi'annahum" is from the root "tabwi'ah" (on the pattern of tadhkirah), meaning to settle with the intention of permanence and duration. [1] Regarding the reality of "trust" (tawakkul) and its philosophy, we had a detailed discussion in Vol. 10, p. 295 onwards (under verse 12 of Surah Ibrahim), and regarding "patience" (sabr), more discussion was provided in Vol. 10, p. 193 and Vol. 6, p. 305.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٦١)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth, and who has disposed the sun and the moon?’ They will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then where do they stray? (61)
ٱللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٦٢)
Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes of His servants, and tightens it for him. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (62)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّن نَّزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَوْتِهَا لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٣)
And if you ask them, ‘Who sends down water from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death?’ They will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah!’ But most of them do not exercise their reason. (63)
وَمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ لَهِىَ ٱلْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٦٤)

فَإِذَا رَكِبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ إِذَا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٥)
When they board the ship, they invoke Allah putting exclusive faith in Him, but when He delivers them to land, behold, they ascribe partners [to Him], (65)

being ungrateful for what We have given them! Let them enjoy. Soon they will know! (66)

Commentary

They Speak of God in Their Hearts and Idols with Their Tongues!

In the preceding verses, the discourse was directed at the polytheists who had perceived the truth of Islam but were unwilling to accept faith and migrate due to the fear of their sustenance being cut off. In the verses under discussion, the address is turned toward the Prophet of Islam—and in fact toward all believers—presenting the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) through "Creation," "Lordship," and "Innate Nature" (Fitrah)—that is, through three distinct paths. It reminds them that their destiny is in the hands of the God whose signs they find in the "horizons" and "within themselves," not in the hands of idols, for idols play no role in this matter.

First, it refers to the issue of the creation of the heavens and the earth, drawing upon their inner beliefs, saying: "If you ask them, who created the heavens and the earth? And who has subjected the sun and the moon to His command for the benefit of His servants? They will all answer with one voice: 'Allah'" (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ سَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ).

For it is certain that neither the idol-worshippers nor anyone else says that the creator of the earth and heavens and the subduer of the sun and moon is a handful of stone and wood fashioned by human hands.

In other words: even the idol-worshippers had no doubt regarding the "Unity of the Creator"; they were polytheists in worship. They would say, "We worship idols only so that they may become intermediaries between us and God," as we read in Surah Yunus, verse 18: "وَ يَقُولُونَ هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ" (And they say: These are our intercessors with Allah).

They claimed: We are not worthy of communicating directly with God; we must establish our connection through idols! "ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفى": "We do not worship them except that they may bring us closer to God in rank!" (Az-Zumar, verse 3).

They were oblivious to the fact that there is no distance between the Creator and the creation, and He is closer to us than our jugular vein. Furthermore, if man, who is the pinnacle of creation, cannot establish a relationship with God, what else could possibly serve as his intermediary?

In any case, at the end of the verse, after mentioning this clear proof, it says: "How then are they turned away [from the worship of Allah, the Exalted Creator, to the worship of a handful of stone and wooden idols]?" (فَأَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ).

"يؤفكون" is derived from the root "افك" (pronounced ifk), meaning to turn something away from its real and true form; accordingly, it is also applied to lies and contrary winds.

The expression "يؤفكون" in the passive voice indicates that they lack the power of decision-making, as if they are being dragged toward idol-worship without will! Additionally, the "subjection of the sun and moon" refers to the systems God has established for them, setting them in motion according to these systems for the benefit of mankind.

Then, to emphasize the same meaning—that He is both the Creator and the Provider—it adds: "Allah extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts it for him" (اللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَ يَقْدِرُ لَهُ). The key to sustenance is in His hand, not in the hands of humans or idols.

The reason it was stated in the previous verses that true believers rely only on Him is that the authority over everything is in His hand. So why should they fear declaring their faith or perceive their lives to be in danger from enemies?! And if they imagine that God has the power but is unaware of their state, this is a great mistake, for "Allah is All-Knowing of all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

Is it possible for someone to be the Creator and Regulator, with His grace reaching creatures moment by moment, and yet be unaware of their condition? This is inconceivable.

In the second stage, the discourse is about "Lordly Monotheism" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) and the descent of the primary source of sustenance from God's side. It says: "If you ask them, who sent down water from the sky and thereby gave life to the earth after its death? They will all say with one voice: Allah!" (وَ لَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِها لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ).

This is the inner belief of the polytheists, which they did not even hesitate to express with their tongues, for they recognized God as both the "Creator" and the "Lord and Regulator of the world."

Then it adds: "Say: All praise belongs to Allah" (قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ).

Praise and thanks are for the One from whom all blessings originate. When water—which is the primary source of life and the substance of existence for all living beings—is from Him, it is clear that all other provisions are also from Him.

Therefore, praise and gratitude must also be exclusive to Him, as other deities have no share in this.

Say: Thanks be to God that they themselves confess to these truths.

And say: Thanks be to God that our logic is so vibrant and crushing that no one has the power to invalidate it.

Since the polytheists' dialogues on one hand, and their words and actions on the other, were contradictory, the end of the verse adds: "But most of them do not understand" (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

Otherwise, how could a rational and understanding person speak with such inconsistency? On one hand, they consider God the Creator, Provider, and Regulator of the world, and on the other, they prostrate before idols that have no influence over their destiny.

On one hand, they believe in the Monotheism of the "Creator" and "Lord," and on the other, they speak of polytheism in worship.

It is interesting that it does not say they lack intellect; it says they do not "exercise intellect" (لا يَعْقِلُونَ), meaning they possess it but do not utilize it!

To elevate their thoughts beyond the horizon of this limited life and open wider worlds before their intellectual vision, the next verse describes the nature of worldly life in comparison to the eternal life of the hereafter in a short and very meaningful phrase: "This life of the world is nothing but diversion and play"; nothing is found in it except entertainment and games (وَ ما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَ لَعِبٌ). "And the home of the Hereafter—that is the real life, if they only knew" (وَ إِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

What a beautiful and eloquent expression! For "لهو" (lahw) means diversion—any activity that occupies a person and diverts them from the fundamental issues of life—and "لعب" (la'ib) refers to actions that have a kind of imaginary order for an imaginary goal (play).

In play and games, one becomes a king, another a minister, another a commander of the army, and another a caravan or a thief; after the conflicts, when the game ends, we see that all roles were but a dream and a fantasy.

The Quran says: The life of this world is a kind of diversion and play; people gather and set their hearts on fantasies, then after a few days they scatter and are hidden under the earth, and then everything is consigned to oblivion.

But the true life, in which there is no annihilation, nor pain, suffering, or discomfort, nor fear or anxiety, nor conflict or friction, is only the life of the Hereafter—if only man knew and were a person of reflection and research.

And those who set their hearts on this life and are charmed and delighted by its glitter are nothing more than children, even if many years of their lives have passed.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "حيوان" (hayawan—rhyming with daraban) is, according to a group of commentators and linguists, a noun meaning "life" (it has an infinitive meaning) [^1], indicating that the "home of the Hereafter" is the very essence of life and vitality, as if life gushes from all its dimensions, and there is nothing in it but "life"!

Clearly, the Quran never intends by this expression to negate the value of divine blessings in this world; rather, it seeks to visualize the value of this life against that life through a explicit and clear comparison. Moreover, it warns man not to be a "prisoner" of these blessings, but to become a "master" over them, and never to trade the authentic values of his existence for them.

In the third stage, it turns to human nature (Fitrah) and the manifestation of the light of Monotheism in the most critical states within the human soul, saying through a very vivid example:

"When they board a ship, they call upon Allah, being sincere to Him in religion, and all else vanishes from their sight; but when He delivers them to the land, they again associate partners [with Him]!" (فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ إِذا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ).

Yes, hardships and storms are the grounds for the blossoming of human nature, for the light of Monotheism is hidden within the souls of all humans. Superstitious customs, wrong upbringing, and evil suggestions cast veils over it. However, when storms blow from every side and whirlpools of trouble appear before man, and he finds his hands cut off from all apparent means, he involuntarily turns toward the supernatural world. He clears every polytheistic thought from his heart and, in the furnace of these events—as exemplified by "مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ"—becomes purified from every impurity.

In short: there is always a luminous point within the human heart that is his line of communication with the supernatural world and the closest path to God. False teachings, negligence, and pride—especially during times of health and abundance—cast veils over it, but the storms of events tear these veils and brush away the ashes, and that luminous point becomes manifest.

For this reason, the great leaders of Islam guided those who were deeply mired in doubt regarding the issue of knowing God through this very path.

We have all heard the story of the bewildered man who was caught in doubt regarding the knowledge of God, and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام guided him through this innate and internal path.

He said: "O son of the Messenger of Allah! Guide me to Allah, who is He? For the disputers have overwhelmed me and made me bewildered!" The Imam عليه السلام said to him: "O servant of Allah! Have you ever boarded a ship?" He said, "Yes." He asked, "Has it ever been wrecked where there was no ship to save you and no swimming to suffice you?" He said, "Yes."

He asked, "Did your heart at that moment attach itself to the fact that there is something capable of saving you from your predicament?" He said, "Yes."

Al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "That thing is Allah, the One capable of saving when there is no savior, and of succoring when there is no succor-giver." [^1]

In the final verse under discussion, after mentioning all these arguments for Monotheism and the worship of God, it confronts the opponents with a severe and crushing threat, saying: "Let them deny Our signs and ignore Our blessings, and enjoy the fleeting pleasures for a few days, but soon they will know" where the end of their disbelief and polytheism will lead them, and what a grim fate it will bring upon them. (لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ وَ لِيَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ).

Although the appearance of the verse here is a command to disbelieve and deny the divine signs, it is obvious that the intent is a threat. It is exactly like saying to a criminal oppressor: "Do whatever crime you can, but soon you will taste the bitter result of your actions all at once!"

In such expressions, although the imperative form is used, the goal is threat, not a request.

Interestingly, the phrase "فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ" (soon they will know) is mentioned in an absolute sense; it does not say what they will know, but rather "soon they will know." This absolute nature of the speech is so that its concept remains as broad as possible and the listener's mind is not limited to any boundary—the result of actions, divine punishment, disgrace in both worlds, and every other kind of misery.

Key Points Hardships are the Ground for the Blossoming of Innate Nature

God willing, under verse 30 of Surah Ar-Rum, we will speak in detail about the innate nature of the principle of Monotheism and the knowledge of God. What is necessary to mention here is that the Holy Quran, in numerous verses, speaks of difficulties and hardships as a means for the emergence and manifestation of this human nature:

In one place it says: "وَ ما بِكُمْ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ إِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فَإِلَيْهِ تَجْئَرُونَ ثُمَّ إِذا كَشَفَ الضُّرَّ عَنْكُمْ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْكُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ": "Whatever blessings you have are from Allah, and when affliction touches you, to Him you cry out for help. Then when He removes the affliction from you, at once a party of you associates others with their Lord!" (An-Nahl, 53-54).

In Surah Yunus, this same meaning is presented in another way: "وَ إِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ الضُّرُّ دَعانا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قاعِداً أَوْ قائِماً فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُ مَرَّ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَدْعُنا إِلى‌ ضر مسه": "When affliction touches man, he calls upon Us [lying] on his side or sitting or standing; but when We have removed his affliction from him, he passes on as if he had never called upon Us for an affliction that touched him!" (Yunus, 12).

In Surah Ar-Rum verse 33, Surah Az-Zumar verse 49, and Surah Al-Isra verses 67-69, this same point is also mentioned with other expressions and meaningful indications.

In the verses under discussion, we also read that this group of polytheists, while their hearts are clouded, turn to idols; but when they hasten to a sea journey and storms, whirlpools, and contrary winds surround them, and their ship is tossed like a straw upon the waves in every direction, and their hands are cut off and shortened from everything, the light of Monotheism begins to shine in their hearts. All fabricated deities are set aside, and complete sincerity—albeit a forced and worthless sincerity!—is achieved. But as soon as the storms subside and the normal state returns, the veils fall, and the soul-searing thorns of polytheism and idol-worship appear around this flower.

It might be said that this state of attention is the result of suggestion and intellectual residues from social culture and environmental thoughts.

This statement would be acceptable if this phenomenon were only tested for religious individuals and in religious environments. However, considering that this state arises even for the most stubborn deniers of God and in non-religious societies, it becomes clear that its root lies elsewhere: in the subconscious of man and within his innate nature and essence.

[^1]: This word is originally derived from "حى" (hayy) and was "حييان" (hayayan), where the second ya changed to waw, becoming "حيوان" (hayawan). [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Volume 3, New Edition, page 41.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا حَرَمًا ءَامِنًا وَيُتَخَطَّفُ ٱلنَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ ۚ أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَكْفُرُونَ (٦٧)
Have they not seen that We have appointed a safe sanctuary, while the people are despoiled all around them? Would they then believe in falsehood and be ungrateful toward the blessing of Allah? (67)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٨)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies the truth when it comes to him? Is not the [final] abode of the faithless in hell? (68)
وَٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمَعَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٦٩)
As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous. (69)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the commentary al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the verse under discussion, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas as follows:

"A group of polytheists said: 'O Muhammad! The only reason we do not enter your religion is that we fear people will snatch us away (and quickly destroy us) because our numbers are few while the Arab polytheists are many. As soon as news reaches them that we have entered your religion, they will swiftly snatch us up, and we would be but a single meal for one of them!' At this point, the verse 'Have they not seen...' was revealed to answer them."

Commentary

In previous verses, this excuse of the polytheists was mentioned in another form—that they feared if they declared their faith and subsequently migrated, their livelihoods would be disrupted—to which the Quran responded in various ways.

In the verses under discussion, the Quran answers them through another approach, saying:

"Have they not seen that We have made a secure sanctuary" (the pure and holy land of Makkah) (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنا حَرَماً آمِناً).

While the Arabs live in insecurity, and "men are being snatched away from around them," everywhere there is looting and killing, yet this land remains safe and secure (وَ يُتَخَطَّفُ النَّاسُ مِنْ حَوْلِهِمْ).

The God who is capable of making the sanctuary of Makkah like a calm and safe island in this turbulent and stormy sea of the Hijaz—how could He not have the power to protect them against their enemies? And how can they fear these weak and helpless people more than the Great and Almighty Allah?! "Do they then believe in falsehood and deny the favor of Allah?"

(أَ فَبِالْباطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَ بِنِعْمَةِ اللَّهِ يَكْفُرُونَ).

In short, the God who can bring security to a small region in the midst of a vast, crisis-ridden land inhabited by semi-savage people—how can He not protect the believers in the midst of a multitude of disbelievers?

After mentioning this clear proof, the next verse draws a conclusion and summary: "And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie against Allah, or denies the truth when it has come to him?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُ).

We have established clear proofs for you that none is worthy of worship except "Allah," yet you invent lies against Allah and devise partners for Him, even claiming that this is a Divine program! On the other hand, We revealed to you a Quran in which the signs of truth are manifest, yet you ignored all of them and cast them behind your backs. Can any injustice be imagined greater than this? It is injustice against oneself and injustice against all humanity, for polytheism and disbelief are a great injustice.

In other words, is injustice in the broad sense of the word anything other than deviation and removing something from its rightful place? Can anything be worse than a human placing a handful of worthless stones and wood on the same level as the Creator of the heavens and the earth?

Furthermore, polytheism is the leaven of all social vices, and other injustices actually stem from it. Worship of whims, worship of status, and worship of the world are each a type of polytheism.

But know that a grim end awaits the polytheists: "Is there not in Hell an abode for the disbelievers?" (أَ لَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْكافِرِينَ).

It is noteworthy that in 15 instances in the Holy Quran, certain individuals are introduced as "the most unjust of people," all of which begin with the interrogative phrase "Who is more unjust" (a rhetorical question implying negation).

A careful study of these verses shows that although they seemingly address diverse issues, they all return to the root of polytheism; therefore, there is no contradiction between them (for further explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 183, under verse 21 of Surah Al-An'am).

The last verse under discussion, which is also the final verse of Surah Al-Ankabut, expresses an important reality that is the essence of this entire Surah and is in harmony with its beginning.

It states: Although the path of Allah has many difficulties—difficulty in terms of recognizing the truth, difficulty in terms of the temptations of the devils among jinn and men, difficulty in terms of the opposition of stubborn and ruthless enemies, and difficulty in terms of potential slips—there is a reality here that gives you strength and assurance against these difficulties and supports you, and that is: "And those who strive in Our cause, We will surely guide them to Our ways, and indeed, Allah is with the doers of good" (وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

Regarding what is meant by "Jihad" (striving) here—whether it is Jihad against the enemy, Jihad against the self, or Jihad in the path of knowing God through scientific and logical means—commentators have offered several possibilities.

Similarly, regarding the expression "in Us" (fina), does it mean in the path of Allah's pleasure, or in the path of Jihad against the self, or in the path of worship, or in the path of fighting the enemy?

However, it is evident that both the term "Jihad" is a broad and absolute term, and the expression "in Us" is as well. Therefore, it includes every kind of Jihad and effort carried out in the way of Allah, for Him, and for the purpose of reaching Divine goals—whether it be in the path of gaining knowledge, Jihad against the self, fighting the enemy, patience in obedience, steadfastness against the temptation of sin, helping the oppressed, or performing any other good deed.

Those who strive in these paths in any shape or form for Allah are subject to Divine support and guidance.

Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that "ways" (subul) here refers to the various paths that lead to Allah: the path of Jihad against the self, the path of fighting enemies, the path of science, knowledge, and culture; in short, Jihad in any of these paths causes guidance to the route that ends with Allah.

This is a promise that Allah has emphatically given to all strivers in His way, reinforcing it with various types of emphasis (the Lam of emphasis and the Nun of heavy emphasis), and He has made victory, progress, and success conditional upon two things: "Jihad" and "Sincerity of Intention."

A group of philosophers believes that thought, study, and reflection do not create knowledge, but rather prepare the human soul to receive intellectual forms. When the human soul becomes ready to receive, the grace of knowledge is sprinkled upon the human soul from the Almighty Creator and the Giver of Forms.

Therefore, man must strive in this path, but guidance is in the hand of Allah.

The hadith stating that knowledge is not through much teaching and learning, but rather it is a light that Allah casts into the heart of whomever He wills and deems worthy, may also refer to this same meaning.

Key Points

1- "Jihad" and "Sincerity"

From the above verse, it is clearly understood that any failure or setback we suffer is due to one of two things: either we have fallen short in Jihad, or there was no sincerity in our work. If these two are combined, according to the emphatic Divine promise, victory and guidance are certain.

If we reflect correctly, we can find the source of the problems and calamities of Islamic societies in this very matter.

Why are the leading Muslims of yesterday the backward ones of today?

Why do they stretch out the hand of need to strangers for everything, even for their culture and laws?

Why must they rely on others to protect themselves against political storms and military invasions?

Why was it that one day others were the beneficiaries of their table of knowledge and culture, but today they must sit at the table of others?

And finally, why are they captives in the hands of others and their lands occupied by aggressors?! All these "whys" have one answer: either they have forgotten Jihad, or their intentions have become tainted.

Yes, Jihad in the scientific, cultural, political, economic, and military arenas has been forgotten. Self-love, love of the world, seeking comfort, short-sightedness, and personal motives have overcome them, to the point that those killed by their own hands are more than those the enemy takes as victims! The self-loss of a group infatuated with the West or the East, the self-selling of a group of rulers and leaders, and the despair and isolation of scholars and thinkers have taken both Jihad and sincerity away from them.

Whenever a small amount of sincerity appears in our ranks and our strivers rouse themselves, victories follow one after another.

The chains of captivity are broken.

Despair turns into hope, failures into success, humiliation into honor and pride, and fragmentation and hypocrisy into unity and cohesion. How great and inspiring is the Quran, which has expressed both the pain and the cure in one short sentence! Yes, those who strive in the way of Allah are subject to Divine guidance, and it is obvious that with His guidance, misguidance and defeat have no meaning.

If we see in some narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام that this verse is interpreted as referring to the Family of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and their followers, it is an expression of a perfect instance of it, because they were the pioneers and leaders in the path of Jihad and sincerity, and it will never be a proof for limiting the concept of the verse.

In any case, every person clearly feels this Quranic truth in their efforts and endeavors: that when they rise for effort and struggle for Allah and in His way, doors are opened to them, and difficulties become easy and hardships become bearable and eventually resolved.

2- People are of Three Groups:

One group consists of stubborn deniers for whom no guidance is of benefit.

Another group consists of sincere strivers who reach the truth.

But the third group is even higher than these; they are not far so as to become near, they are not separate from Him so as to join Him, for they are always with Him! The previous verse ("And who is more unjust than he who invents...") referred to the first group.

The phrase "And those who strive in Us" refers to the second group.

And the phrase "Indeed, Allah is with the doers of good" refers to the third group!

Additionally, it becomes clear from this expression that the status of the "doers of good" (muhsinin) is higher than that of the "strivers" (mujahidin), because in addition to Jihad and effort in the way of their own salvation, they possess the status of altruism and excellence and strive for others as well.

O Lord! Grant us such success that throughout our lives we never cease from effort and striving in Your way. O Allah! Grant us such sincerity that we think of none but You, and take no step for other than You.

O God! Raise us above the status of the strivers, and let us attain the status of excellence and altruism of the doers of good, and keep us under the cover of Your guidance throughout our lives! Amen, O Lord of the Worlds!

End of Surah Al-Ankabut 21 Shawwal 1403 - 10 Mordad 1362
